[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Location_map_of_the_United_Kingdom_and_the_European_Union.svg/250px-Location_map_of_the_United_Kingdom_and_the_European_Union.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Location_map_of_the_United_Kingdom_and_the_European_Union.svg)

The United Kingdom in orange; the European Union (27 member states) in blue: a representation of the result of Brexit

**Brexit** ([/ˈbrɛksɪt, ˈbrɛɡzɪt/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English");[[1]](#cite_note-1) a [portmanteau](/wiki/Portmanteau "Portmanteau") of "Britain" and "Exit") was the [withdrawal](/wiki/Withdrawal_from_the_European_Union "Withdrawal from the European Union") of the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") (UK) from the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") (EU).

Brexit took place at 23:00 [GMT](/wiki/GMT "GMT") on 31 January 2020 (00:00 1 February 2020 [CET](/wiki/Central_European_Time "Central European Time")).[[a]](#cite_note-2) The UK, which joined the EU precursor, the [European Communities](/wiki/European_Communities "European Communities") (EC), on 1 January 1973, is the only member state to have withdrawn, although previously the territories of [Algeria](/wiki/Algeria "Algeria") ceased to be part of the EC following its independence from the member state France in 1962 and [Greenland](/wiki/Greenland "Greenland") (part of the [Kingdom of Denmark](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Denmark "Kingdom of Denmark")) left the EC in 1985. Following Brexit, [EU law](/wiki/EU_law "EU law") and the [Court of Justice of the European Union](/wiki/Court_of_Justice_of_the_European_Union "Court of Justice of the European Union") no longer have [primacy over British law](/wiki/Primacy_of_European_Union_law "Primacy of European Union law") but the UK remains bound by obligations in treaties it has with other countries around the world, including many with EU member states and with the EU itself. The [European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018](/wiki/European_Union_(Withdrawal)_Act_2018 "European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018") retains relevant EU law as [domestic law](/wiki/Law_of_the_United_Kingdom "Law of the United Kingdom"), which the UK can amend or repeal.

The EU and its institutions developed gradually after their establishment. Throughout the period of British membership, [Eurosceptic](/wiki/Euroscepticism_in_the_United_Kingdom "Euroscepticism in the United Kingdom") groups had existed in the UK, opposing aspects of the EU and its predecessors. The [Labour](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") prime minister [Harold Wilson](/wiki/Harold_Wilson "Harold Wilson")'s pro-EC government held [a referendum on continued EC membership in 1975](/wiki/1975_United_Kingdom_European_Communities_membership_referendum "1975 United Kingdom European Communities membership referendum"), in which 67.2% voted to stay. Despite growing political opposition by a minority of UK politicians to further [European integration](/wiki/European_integration "European integration") aimed at "[ever closer union](/wiki/Ever_closer_union "Ever closer union")" between 1975–2016, from factions of the [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") in the 1980s–2000s, no further referendums on the issue were held.

By the mid-2010s, the growing popularity of the [UK Independence Party](/wiki/UK_Independence_Party "UK Independence Party") (UKIP), as well as pressure from Eurosceptics within his own party, persuaded the Conservative prime minister [David Cameron](/wiki/David_Cameron "David Cameron") to promise a referendum on British membership of the EU if his government was re-elected. Following the [2015 general election](/wiki/2015_United_Kingdom_general_election "2015 United Kingdom general election"), which produced a small but unexpected majority for the governing Conservative Party, the promised [referendum on continued EU membership](/wiki/2016_United_Kingdom_European_Union_membership_referendum "2016 United Kingdom European Union membership referendum") was held on 23 June 2016. Supporters of the [Remain](/wiki/Britain_Stronger_in_Europe "Britain Stronger in Europe") campaign included then-prime minister David Cameron, the future prime ministers [Theresa May](/wiki/Theresa_May "Theresa May"), [Liz Truss](/wiki/Liz_Truss "Liz Truss"), and [Sir Keir Starmer](/wiki/Sir_Keir_Starmer "Sir Keir Starmer"), and the ex–prime ministers [John Major](/wiki/John_Major "John Major"), [Tony Blair](/wiki/Tony_Blair "Tony Blair"), and [Gordon Brown](/wiki/Gordon_Brown "Gordon Brown"); supporters of the [Leave](/wiki/Vote_Leave "Vote Leave") campaign included the future prime ministers [Boris Johnson](/wiki/Boris_Johnson "Boris Johnson") and [Rishi Sunak](/wiki/Rishi_Sunak "Rishi Sunak"). The electorate voted to Leave the EU by a slight margin, with a 51.9% share of the vote, all regions of [England](/wiki/England "England") and [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales") except [London](/wiki/Greater_London "Greater London") voting in favour of Brexit, and [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") and [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland") voting to remain. The result led to Cameron's sudden resignation, his replacement by former Home Secretary Theresa May, and four years of negotiations with the EU over the terms of departure and future relations, completed under a [Boris Johnson-led government](/wiki/First_Johnson_ministry "First Johnson ministry"), with the Conservative Party in office.

The negotiation was both politically challenging and deeply divisive, leading to two snap general elections in [2017](/wiki/2017_United_Kingdom_general_election "2017 United Kingdom general election") and [2019](/wiki/2019_United_Kingdom_general_election "2019 United Kingdom general election"). One proposal under the [second May ministry](/wiki/Second_May_ministry "Second May ministry") was overwhelmingly rejected by the [UK Parliament](/wiki/UK_Parliament "UK Parliament"), causing great uncertainty and leading to postponement of the withdrawal date to avoid a [no-deal Brexit](/wiki/No-deal_Brexit "No-deal Brexit"). The UK officially left the European Union on 31 January 2020 after a withdrawal deal was passed by Parliament, but continued to participate in many EU institutions (including the single market and customs union) during an [eleven-month transition period](/wiki/Brexit_withdrawal_agreement "Brexit withdrawal agreement") during which it was hoped that details of the post-Brexit relationship could be agreed and implemented. [Trade deal negotiations](/wiki/Trade_negotiation_between_the_UK_and_the_EU "Trade negotiation between the UK and the EU") continued within days of the scheduled end of the transition period, and the [EU–UK Trade and Cooperation Agreement](/wiki/EU%E2%80%93UK_Trade_and_Cooperation_Agreement "EU–UK Trade and Cooperation Agreement") was signed on 30 December 2020. The effects of Brexit in the UK are in part determined by the cooperation agreement, which [provisionally applied](/wiki/Provisional_application_(treaty) "Provisional application (treaty)") from 1 January 2021, until it formally came into force on 1 May 2021.[[2]](#cite_note-CouncilRatifies-3)

## Timeline

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Timeline")]

Main article: [Timeline of Brexit](/wiki/Timeline_of_Brexit "Timeline of Brexit")

Following a UK-wide [referendum on 23 June 2016](/wiki/2016_United_Kingdom_European_Union_membership_referendum "2016 United Kingdom European Union membership referendum"), in which 51.89 per cent voted in favour of leaving the EU and 48.11 per cent voted to remain a member state, [David Cameron](/wiki/David_Cameron "David Cameron") resigned as prime minister. On 29 March 2017, the new [British government](/wiki/British_government "British government") led by [Theresa May](/wiki/Theresa_May "Theresa May") chose to formally notify the EU of the country's intention to withdraw from the EU in two years, despite there being no agreement among UK politicians on objectives for post-Brexit relations with the EU. The withdrawal, originally scheduled for 29 March 2019, was subsequently delayed by the [deadlock in the British parliament](/wiki/Parliamentary_votes_on_Brexit "Parliamentary votes on Brexit") after the [June 2017 general election](/wiki/2017_United_Kingdom_general_election "2017 United Kingdom general election"), which resulted in a [hung parliament](/wiki/Hung_parliament "Hung parliament") in which the [Conservatives](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") lost their majority but remained the largest party. This deadlock eventually led to three extensions of the [UK's Article 50 process](/wiki/United_Kingdom_invocation_of_Article_50_of_the_Treaty_on_European_Union "United Kingdom invocation of Article 50 of the Treaty on European Union").

The deadlock was resolved after a [subsequent general election was held in December 2019](/wiki/2019_United_Kingdom_general_election "2019 United Kingdom general election"). In that election, Conservatives who campaigned in support of a "hard-brexit" withdrawal agreement led by [Boris Johnson](/wiki/Boris_Johnson "Boris Johnson") won an overall majority of 80 seats. After the December 2019 election, the British parliament finally ratified the [withdrawal agreement](/wiki/Withdrawal_agreement "Withdrawal agreement") with the [European Union (Withdrawal Agreement) Act 2020](/wiki/European_Union_(Withdrawal_Agreement)_Act_2020 "European Union (Withdrawal Agreement) Act 2020"). The UK left the EU at the end of 31 January 2020 [CET](/wiki/Central_European_Time "Central European Time") (11 p.m. [GMT](/wiki/GMT "GMT")).[[3]](#cite_note-4) This began a transition period that ended on 31 December 2020 CET (11 p.m. GMT), during which the UK and EU negotiated their future relationship.[[4]](#cite_note-Bennett-5) During the transition, the UK remained subject to [EU law](/wiki/EU_law "EU law") and remained part of the [European Union Customs Union](/wiki/European_Union_Customs_Union "European Union Customs Union") and the [European single market](/wiki/European_single_market "European single market"). However, it was no longer part of the EU's political bodies or institutions.[[5]](#cite_note-Edgington-6)[[6]](#cite_note-QAEC-7)

The withdrawal had been advocated by mostly right-wing and conservative [hard Eurosceptics](/wiki/Euroscepticism#Hard_Euroscepticism "Euroscepticism") and opposed by [pro-Europeanists](/wiki/Pro-Europeanists "Pro-Europeanists") mostly from the rest of the political spectrum. [In 1973, the UK joined](/wiki/Accession_of_the_United_Kingdom_to_the_European_Communities "Accession of the United Kingdom to the European Communities") the [European Communities](/wiki/European_Communities "European Communities") (EC) – principally the [European Economic Community](/wiki/European_Economic_Community "European Economic Community") (EEC) – and its continued membership was endorsed in the [1975 membership referendum](/wiki/1975_United_Kingdom_European_Communities_membership_referendum "1975 United Kingdom European Communities membership referendum"). In the 1970s and 1980s, withdrawal from the EC was advocated mainly by the political left, e.g. in the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)")'s 1983 election manifesto. The 1992 [Maastricht Treaty](/wiki/Maastricht_Treaty "Maastricht Treaty"), which founded the EU, was [ratified by the British parliament in 1993](/wiki/European_Communities_(Amendment)_Act_1993 "European Communities (Amendment) Act 1993") but was not put to a referendum. The Eurosceptic wing of the Conservative Party led [a rebellion](/wiki/Maastricht_Rebels "Maastricht Rebels") over the ratification of the treaty and, with the [UK Independence Party](/wiki/UK_Independence_Party "UK Independence Party") (UKIP) and the cross-party [People's Pledge](/wiki/People%27s_Pledge "People's Pledge") campaign, then led a collective campaign, particularly after the [Treaty of Lisbon](/wiki/Treaty_of_Lisbon "Treaty of Lisbon") was also ratified by the [European Union (Amendment) Act 2008](/wiki/European_Union_(Amendment)_Act_2008 "European Union (Amendment) Act 2008") without being put to a referendum following a previous promise to hold [a referendum](/wiki/European_Union_Bill_2004%E2%80%932005 "European Union Bill 2004–2005") on ratifying the abandoned [European Constitution](/wiki/European_Constitution "European Constitution"), which was never held. After promising to hold a second membership referendum if his government was elected, Conservative prime minister David Cameron held this referendum in 2016. Cameron, who had campaigned to remain, resigned after the result and was succeeded by [Theresa May](/wiki/Theresa_May "Theresa May").

On 29 March 2017, the British government formally began the withdrawal process by invoking Article 50 of the [Treaty on European Union](/wiki/Treaty_on_European_Union "Treaty on European Union") with [permission from Parliament](/wiki/European_Union_(Notification_of_Withdrawal)_Act_2017 "European Union (Notification of Withdrawal) Act 2017"). May called [a snap general election](/wiki/2017_United_Kingdom_general_election "2017 United Kingdom general election") in June 2017, which resulted in a Conservative minority government [supported](/wiki/Confidence_and_supply "Confidence and supply") by the [Democratic Unionist Party](/wiki/Democratic_Unionist_Party "Democratic Unionist Party") (DUP). UK–EU withdrawal negotiations began later that month. The UK negotiated to leave the EU customs union and single market. This resulted in the November 2018 [withdrawal agreement](/wiki/Withdrawal_agreement "Withdrawal agreement"), but the British parliament [voted against ratifying it](/wiki/Parliamentary_votes_on_Brexit "Parliamentary votes on Brexit") three times. The Labour Party wanted any agreement to maintain a customs union, while many Conservatives opposed the agreement's [financial settlement](/wiki/Brexit_divorce_bill "Brexit divorce bill"), as well as the "[Irish backstop](/wiki/Irish_backstop "Irish backstop")" designed to prevent border controls between [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland") and the [Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland"). The [Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)"), [Scottish National Party](/wiki/Scottish_National_Party "Scottish National Party") (SNP), and others sought to reverse Brexit through [a proposed second referendum](/wiki/Proposed_referendum_on_the_Brexit_withdrawal_agreement "Proposed referendum on the Brexit withdrawal agreement").

On 14 March 2019, the British parliament voted for May to ask the EU to delay Brexit until June, and then later October.[[7]](#cite_note-Parliament_UK-8) Having failed to get her agreement approved, May resigned as prime minister in July and was succeeded by [Boris Johnson](/wiki/Boris_Johnson "Boris Johnson"). He sought to replace parts of the agreement and vowed to leave the EU by the new deadline. On 17 October 2019, the British government and the EU agreed on a revised withdrawal agreement, with new arrangements for Northern Ireland.[[8]](#cite_note-Europa17oct19-9)[[9]](#cite_note-BBC_News-10) Parliament approved the agreement for further scrutiny, but rejected passing it into law before the 31 October deadline, and forced the government (through the "[Benn Act](/wiki/Benn_Act "Benn Act")") to ask for a third Brexit delay. [An early general election](/wiki/2019_United_Kingdom_general_election "2019 United Kingdom general election") was then held on 12 December. The Conservatives won a large majority in that election, with Johnson declaring that the UK would leave the EU in early 2020.[[10]](#cite_note-11) The withdrawal agreement was ratified by the UK on 23 January and by the EU on 30 January; it came into force on 31 January 2020.[[11]](#cite_note-Guardian2020jan24-12)[[12]](#cite_note-sparrow1-13)[[13]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-14)

## Terminology and etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Terminology and etymology")]

Main article: [Glossary of Brexit terms](/wiki/Glossary_of_Brexit_terms "Glossary of Brexit terms")

Following the referendum of 23 June 2016, many new pieces of Brexit-related [jargon](/wiki/Jargon "Jargon") entered popular use.[[14]](#cite_note-15)[[15]](#cite_note-16) The word *[Brexit](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Brexit "wiktionary:Brexit")* is a portmanteau of the phrase "British exit".[[16]](#cite_note-Lewis-Hargreave-17) According to the *[Oxford English Dictionary](/wiki/Oxford_English_Dictionary "Oxford English Dictionary")*, the term was coined in a blog post on the website [Euractiv](/wiki/Euractiv "Euractiv") by Peter Wilding, director of European policy at [BSkyB](/wiki/BSkyB "BSkyB"), on 15 May 2012.[[17]](#cite_note-Tempest-18) Wilding coined *Brexit* to refer to the end of the UK's membership of the EU; by 2016, usage of the word had increased by 3,400% in one year.[[18]](#cite_note-Davis-19) On 2 November 2016, the *[Collins English Dictionary](/wiki/Collins_English_Dictionary "Collins English Dictionary")* selected *Brexit* as the [word of the year](/wiki/Word_of_the_year#Collins_English_Dictionary "Word of the year") for 2016.[[19]](#cite_note-Flood-20)

## Background: the United Kingdom and EC/EU membership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Background: the United Kingdom and EC/EU membership")]

Main articles: [Accession of the United Kingdom to the European Communities](/wiki/Accession_of_the_United_Kingdom_to_the_European_Communities "Accession of the United Kingdom to the European Communities") and [United Kingdom membership of the European Union](/wiki/United_Kingdom_membership_of_the_European_Union "United Kingdom membership of the European Union")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Inner_Six_and_Outer_Seven.svg/250px-Inner_Six_and_Outer_Seven.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Inner_Six_and_Outer_Seven.svg)

The [Inner Six](/wiki/Inner_Six "Inner Six") (blue) and [Outer Seven](/wiki/Outer_Seven "Outer Seven") (green) of [European integration](/wiki/European_integration "European integration") from 1961 until 1973:

EC Members (Inner Six)

EFTA Members (Outer Seven)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/EC09-1973_European_Community_map.svg/250px-EC09-1973_European_Community_map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:EC09-1973_European_Community_map.svg)

When the UK first joined the [European Communities](/wiki/European_Communities "European Communities") (along with [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark") and [Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland")) on 1 January 1973 it was one of just nine member states that made up the bloc at the time.

EC Members

The "[Inner Six](/wiki/Inner_Six "Inner Six")" European countries signed the [Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1951) "Treaty of Paris (1951)") in 1951, establishing the [European Coal and Steel Community](/wiki/European_Coal_and_Steel_Community "European Coal and Steel Community") (ECSC). The 1955 [Messina Conference](/wiki/Messina_Conference "Messina Conference") deemed that the ECSC was a success, and resolved to extend the concept further, thereby leading to the 1957 [Treaties of Rome](/wiki/Treaties_of_Rome "Treaties of Rome") establishing the [European Economic Community](/wiki/European_Economic_Community "European Economic Community") (EEC) and the [European Atomic Energy Community](/wiki/European_Atomic_Energy_Community "European Atomic Energy Community") (Euratom). In [1967](/wiki/Merger_Treaty "Merger Treaty"), these became known as the European Communities (EC). The UK attempted to join in 1963 and 1967, but these applications were vetoed by the [president of France](/wiki/President_of_France "President of France"), [Charles de Gaulle](/wiki/Charles_de_Gaulle "Charles de Gaulle"), who feared the UK would be a [Trojan Horse](/wiki/Trojan_Horse "Trojan Horse") for US influence.[[20]](#cite_note-21)[[21]](#cite_note-22)

Some time after de Gaulle resigned in 1969, the UK successfully applied for [European Communities](/wiki/European_Communities "European Communities") (EC) membership. Membership of the then EEC was thoroughly discussed at the long debate in the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") in October 1971. It led to the decisive vote in favour of membership by 356 to 244. As historian Piers Ludlow observed, the 1971 parliamentary debate was of high quality and considered all issues. The British were not "misled and persuaded to accept membership in a narrow commercial entity without being aware that the EEC was a political project liable to develop in the future".[[22]](#cite_note-23) The [Conservative](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") prime minister [Edward Heath](/wiki/Edward_Heath "Edward Heath") signed the [Treaty of Accession](/wiki/Treaty_of_Accession_1972 "Treaty of Accession 1972") in 1972.[[23]](#cite_note-24) Parliament passed the [European Communities Act](/wiki/European_Communities_Act_1972_(UK) "European Communities Act 1972 (UK)") later that year[[24]](#cite_note-25) and the UK joined [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark") and the [Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland") in [becoming a member](/wiki/Accession_of_the_United_Kingdom_to_the_European_Communities "Accession of the United Kingdom to the European Communities") on 1 January 1973, without referendum.[[25]](#cite_note-26)

During the 1970s and 1980s, the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") was the more Eurosceptic of the two major parties, and the Conservatives the more Europhile. Labour won the [February 1974 general election](/wiki/February_1974_United_Kingdom_general_election "February 1974 United Kingdom general election") without a majority and then contested the subsequent [October 1974 general election](/wiki/October_1974_United_Kingdom_general_election "October 1974 United Kingdom general election") with a commitment to renegotiate Britain's terms of membership of the EC, believing them to be unfavourable, and then hold a [referendum](/wiki/Referendum "Referendum") on whether to remain in the EC on the new terms.[[26]](#cite_note-27) Labour again won the election (this time with a small majority), and in 1975 the UK held its [first ever national referendum](/wiki/1975_United_Kingdom_European_Communities_membership_referendum "1975 United Kingdom European Communities membership referendum"), asking whether the UK should remain in the EC. Despite significant division within the ruling Labour Party,[[27]](#cite_note-28) all major political parties and the mainstream press supported continuing membership of the EC. On 5 June 1975, 67.2% of the electorate and all but two[[28]](#cite_note-29) British counties and regions voted to stay in;[[29]](#cite_note-30) support for the UK to leave the EC in 1975 appears unrelated to the support for Leave in the 2016 referendum.[[30]](#cite_note-31)

In 1979, the UK secured its first [opt-out](/wiki/Opt-outs_in_the_European_Union "Opt-outs in the European Union"), although the expression was not contemporary; it was the only EEC country not to take part in the [European Monetary System](/wiki/European_Monetary_System "European Monetary System").

The Labour Party campaigned in the [1983 general election](/wiki/1983_United_Kingdom_general_election "1983 United Kingdom general election") on a commitment to withdraw from the EC without a referendum.[[31]](#cite_note-foot-32) Following their heavy defeat in that election, Labour changed its policy.[[31]](#cite_note-foot-32) In 1985, the [second Margaret Thatcher government](/wiki/Second_Thatcher_ministry "Second Thatcher ministry") ratified the [Single European Act](/wiki/Single_European_Act "Single European Act")—the first major revision to the [Treaty of Rome](/wiki/Treaty_of_Rome "Treaty of Rome")—without a referendum.[[32]](#cite_note-33)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/United_Kingdom_European_Communities_membership_referendum%2C_1975_compared_to_United_Kingdom_European_Union_membership_referendum%2C_2016.svg/500px-United_Kingdom_European_Communities_membership_referendum%2C_1975_compared_to_United_Kingdom_European_Union_membership_referendum%2C_2016.svg.png)](/wiki/File:United_Kingdom_European_Communities_membership_referendum,_1975_compared_to_United_Kingdom_European_Union_membership_referendum,_2016.svg)

Comparison of results of 1975 and 2016 referendums

In October 1990, under pressure from senior ministers and despite Thatcher's deep reservations, the UK joined the [European Exchange Rate Mechanism](/wiki/European_Exchange_Rate_Mechanism "European Exchange Rate Mechanism") (ERM), with the [pound sterling](/wiki/Pound_sterling "Pound sterling") pegged to the [deutschmark](/wiki/Deutschmark "Deutschmark"). Thatcher resigned as prime minister the following month, amid Conservative Party divisions arising partly from her increasingly Eurosceptic views. The UK was forced to withdraw from the ERM on [Black Wednesday](/wiki/Black_Wednesday "Black Wednesday") in September 1992, after the pound sterling came under pressure from [currency speculation](/wiki/Currency_speculation "Currency speculation").[[33]](#cite_note-34) Italy left the same month, but would soon rejoin on a different band. The UK did not seek re-entry and remained outside the ERM.

On 1 November 1993, after the UK and the other eleven member states had ratified, the EC became the EU under the Maastricht Treaty[[34]](#cite_note-35) compromise between member states seeking deeper integration and those wishing to retain greater national control in the [economic](/wiki/Economic_union "Economic union") and [political union](/wiki/Political_union "Political union").[[35]](#cite_note-36) [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark"), [France](/wiki/France "France"), and the Republic of Ireland held referendums to ratify the Maastricht Treaty. In accordance with [Constitution of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_United_Kingdom "Constitution of the United Kingdom"), specifically that of [parliamentary sovereignty](/wiki/Parliamentary_sovereignty "Parliamentary sovereignty"), ratification in the UK was not subject to approval by referendum. Despite this, British constitutional historian [Vernon Bogdanor](/wiki/Vernon_Bogdanor "Vernon Bogdanor") wrote that there was "a clear constitutional rationale for requiring a referendum" because although MPs are entrusted with legislative power by the electorate, they are not given authority to transfer that power (the UK's previous three referendums all concerned this). Further, as the ratification of the treaty was in the manifestos of the three major political parties, voters opposed to ratification had limited options for expressing this. For Bogdanor, while the ratification by the House of Commons might be legal, it would not be legitimate—which requires popular consent. The way in which the treaty was ratified, he judged, was "likely to have fundamental consequences both for British politics and for Britain's relationship with the [EC]."[[36]](#cite_note-37)[[37]](#cite_note-38)

### Euroscepticism in the United Kingdom and the role of the EU leadership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Euroscepticism in the United Kingdom and the role of the EU leadership")]

Main article: [Euroscepticism in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Euroscepticism_in_the_United_Kingdom "Euroscepticism in the United Kingdom")

[![Margaret Thatcher](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Margaret_Thatcher_%281983%29.jpg/120px-Margaret_Thatcher_%281983%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Margaret_Thatcher_(1983).jpg)

[![Nigel Farage](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Nigel_Farage_%2845718080574%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Nigel_Farage_%2845718080574%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nigel_Farage_(45718080574)_(cropped).jpg)

[![David Cameron](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/David_Cameron_official.jpg/120px-David_Cameron_official.jpg)](/wiki/File:David_Cameron_official.jpg)

[Conservative](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") prime ministers [Margaret Thatcher](/wiki/Margaret_Thatcher "Margaret Thatcher") (*left*) and [David Cameron](/wiki/David_Cameron "David Cameron") (*right*) used [Eurosceptic](/wiki/Eurosceptic "Eurosceptic") rhetoric while supporting the UK's membership and development of the [European single market](/wiki/European_single_market "European single market"). Euroscepticism – and in particular the impact of the [UK Independence Party](/wiki/UK_Independence_Party "UK Independence Party") (former leader [Nigel Farage](/wiki/Nigel_Farage "Nigel Farage") pictured centre) on the Conservatives' election results – contributed to Cameron's [2015–16 attempt to renegotiate the UK's EU membership](/wiki/2015%E2%80%932016_United_Kingdom_renegotiation_of_European_Union_membership "2015–2016 United Kingdom renegotiation of European Union membership"), and ultimately the holding of the [2016 referendum](/wiki/2016_United_Kingdom_European_Union_membership_referendum "2016 United Kingdom European Union membership referendum"). Cameron supported the UK remaining in the EU, but resigned after the referendum produced a victory for the "Leave" campaign.

#### Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Background")]

Thatcher, who had previously supported the common market and the Single European Act, in the [Bruges speech](/wiki/Bruges_speech "Bruges speech") of 1988 warned against "a European super-state exercising a new dominance from Brussels". She influenced [Daniel Hannan](/wiki/Daniel_Hannan "Daniel Hannan"), who in 1990 founded the Oxford Campaign for Independent Britain; "With hindsight, some see this as the start of the campaign for Brexit", the *[Financial Times](/wiki/Financial_Times "Financial Times")* later wrote.[[38]](#cite_note-kuper20190620-39)

The vote to approve the [Maastricht Treaty](/wiki/Maastricht_Treaty "Maastricht Treaty") in 1993 triggered a strong Eurosceptic response, splitting the [Conservative](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") Party and leading to many past supporters forming alternative Eurosceptic parties. This included Sir [James Goldsmith](/wiki/James_Goldsmith "James Goldsmith") forming the [Referendum Party](/wiki/Referendum_Party "Referendum Party") in 1994 to contest the [1997 general election](/wiki/1997_United_Kingdom_general_election "1997 United Kingdom general election") on a platform of providing a referendum on the UK's relationship with the EU.[[39]](#cite_note-40)

#### Role of the EU leadership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Role of the EU leadership")]

Perceptions of EU leadership during major crises significantly contributed to Euroscepticism in the UK. During the eurozone debt crisis, strict austerity measures were imposed as a condition for bailouts, with EU member states, including the UK, asked to contribute to bailout plans. Critics argued that such policies disproportionately served the interests of leading EU nations, particularly Germany, and undermined national sovereignty.[[40]](#cite_note-41)

Similarly, during the 2015 migration crisis, Chancellor [Angela Merkel](/wiki/Angela_Merkel "Angela Merkel")'s decision to open EU borders and her request that member states share the burden of accommodating refugees sparked significant backlash. Many in the UK viewed this as an imposition of obligations without adequate consultation, reinforcing Eurosceptic narratives.[[41]](#cite_note-42)

Populist parties such as [UKIP](/wiki/UKIP "UKIP") exploited these perceptions, linking EU leadership to broader fears of a loss of British sovereignty. Campaign rhetoric often employed nationalist sentiments and portrayed the EU as disproportionately influenced by certain member states.[[42]](#cite_note-43)

#### Electoral success and the 2016 referendum

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Electoral success and the 2016 referendum")]

UKIP's electoral success, driven by its anti-EU campaigns, culminated in significant gains during the [2014 European elections](/wiki/2014_European_Parliament_election_in_the_United_Kingdom "2014 European Parliament election in the United Kingdom"), where it became the largest UK party with 27.5% of the vote.[[43]](#cite_note-44) This success put pressure on the ruling [Conservative](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") Party, ultimately leading to Prime Minister [David Cameron](/wiki/David_Cameron "David Cameron")'s decision to hold the [2016 United Kingdom European Union membership referendum](/wiki/2016_United_Kingdom_European_Union_membership_referendum "2016 United Kingdom European Union membership referendum").

By linking perceived EU leadership overreach to concerns about sovereignty, Eurosceptic parties and media shaped public opinion in the UK, contributing to the referendum outcome.

### Opinion polls 1977–2015

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Opinion polls 1977–2015")]

Main article: [Opinion polling for the United Kingdom European Union membership referendum](/wiki/Opinion_polling_for_the_United_Kingdom_European_Union_membership_referendum "Opinion polling for the United Kingdom European Union membership referendum")

Both pro- and anti-EU views had majority support at different times from 1977 to 2015.[[44]](#cite_note-Mortimore-45) In the [EC membership referendum of 1975](/wiki/1975_United_Kingdom_European_Communities_membership_referendum "1975 United Kingdom European Communities membership referendum"), two-thirds of British voters favoured continued EC membership. Over the decades of UK-EU membership, Euroscepticism existed on both the left and right of British politics.[[45]](#cite_note-46)[[46]](#cite_note-Foster-47)[[47]](#cite_note-48)

According to a statistical analysis published in April 2016 by Professor [John Curtice](/wiki/John_Curtice "John Curtice") of [Strathclyde University](/wiki/Strathclyde_University "Strathclyde University"), surveys showed an increase in Euroscepticism (a wish to leave the EU or stay in the EU and try to reduce the EU's powers) from 38% in 1993 to 65% in 2015. The BSA survey for the period of July–November 2015 showed that 60% backed the option to continue as a member and 30% backed withdrawal.[[48]](#cite_note-49)

## 2016 EU membership referendum

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2016 EU membership referendum")]

Main article: [2016 United Kingdom European Union membership referendum](/wiki/2016_United_Kingdom_European_Union_membership_referendum "2016 United Kingdom European Union membership referendum")

### Negotiations for membership reform

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Negotiations for membership reform")]

Main article: [2015–2016 United Kingdom renegotiation of European Union membership](/wiki/2015%E2%80%932016_United_Kingdom_renegotiation_of_European_Union_membership "2015–2016 United Kingdom renegotiation of European Union membership")

In 2012, Prime Minister [David Cameron](/wiki/David_Cameron "David Cameron") initially rejected calls for a referendum on the UK's EU membership,[[49]](#cite_note-50) but then suggested the possibility of a future referendum to endorse his proposed renegotiation of Britain's relationship with the rest of the EU.[[50]](#cite_note-51) According to the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC"), "The prime minister acknowledged the need to ensure the UK's [renegotiated] position within the [EU] had 'the full-hearted support of the British people' but they needed to show 'tactical and strategic patience'."[[51]](#cite_note-52) On 23 January 2013, under pressure from many of his MPs and from the rise of UKIP, Cameron promised in his [Bloomberg speech](/wiki/Bloomberg_speech "Bloomberg speech") that a Conservative government would hold an in-or-out referendum on EU membership before the end of 2017, on a renegotiated package, if elected in the [7 May 2015 general election](/wiki/2015_United_Kingdom_general_election "2015 United Kingdom general election").[[52]](#cite_note-53) This was included in the Conservative Party manifesto for the election.[[53]](#cite_note-54)[[54]](#cite_note-55)

The [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") won the election with a majority. Soon afterwards, the [European Union Referendum Act 2015](/wiki/European_Union_Referendum_Act_2015 "European Union Referendum Act 2015") was introduced into [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") to enable the referendum. Cameron favoured remaining in a reformed EU, and sought to renegotiate on four key points: protection of the single market for non-eurozone countries, reduction of "red tape", exempting Britain from "ever-closer union", and restricting immigration from the rest of the EU.[[55]](#cite_note-BBC_News_Nov_2015-56)

In December 2015, opinion polls showed a clear majority in favour of remaining in the EU; they also showed support would drop if Cameron did not negotiate adequate safeguards[*[definition needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] for non-eurozone member states, and restrictions on benefits for non-UK EU citizens.[[56]](#cite_note-57)

The outcome of the renegotiations was revealed in February 2016. Some limits to in-work benefits for new EU immigrants were agreed, but before they could be applied, a member state such as the UK would have to get permission from the [European Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission") and then from the [European Council](/wiki/European_Council "European Council"), which is composed of the heads of government of every member state.[[57]](#cite_note-FF1-58)

In a speech to the House of Commons on 22 February 2016, Cameron announced a referendum date of 23 June 2016, and commented on the renegotiation settlement.[[58]](#cite_note-Hansard_22_February_2016_Column_21-25-59) He spoke of an intention to trigger the Article 50 process immediately following a Leave vote and of the "two-year time period to negotiate the arrangements for exit."[[59]](#cite_note-60)

After the original wording for the referendum question was challenged,[[60]](#cite_note-Three_years_since_the_Brexit_vote,_meet_the_man_behind_Leave_and_Remain-61) the government agreed to change the official referendum question to "Should the United Kingdom remain a member of the European Union or leave the European Union?"

### Referendum result

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Referendum result")]

Main article: [Results of the 2016 United Kingdom European Union membership referendum](/wiki/Results_of_the_2016_United_Kingdom_European_Union_membership_referendum "Results of the 2016 United Kingdom European Union membership referendum")

In the referendum 51.89% voted in favour of leaving the EU (Leave), and 48.11% voted in favour of remaining a member of the EU (Remain).[[61]](#cite_note-BBC24forecast-62)[[62]](#cite_note-63) After this result, Cameron resigned on 13 July 2016, with [Theresa May](/wiki/Theresa_May "Theresa May") becoming Prime Minister after a [leadership contest](/wiki/2016_Conservative_Party_leadership_election "2016 Conservative Party leadership election"). A [petition calling for a second referendum](/wiki/EU_Referendum_Rules_triggering_a_2nd_EU_Referendum "EU Referendum Rules triggering a 2nd EU Referendum") attracted more than four million signatures,[[63]](#cite_note-64)[[64]](#cite_note-65) but was rejected by the government on 9 July.[[65]](#cite_note-66)

|  | [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales") | 2,270,272 | 71.7% | 772,347 | **854,572** | 47.47% | **52.53%** | 1,135 |

### Voter demographics and trends

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Voter demographics and trends")]

Further information: [Causes of the vote in favour of Brexit](/wiki/Causes_of_the_vote_in_favour_of_Brexit "Causes of the vote in favour of Brexit")

A 2017 study published in the journal *[Economic Policy](/wiki/Economic_Policy "Economic Policy")* showed that the Leave vote tended to be greater in areas which had lower incomes and high [unemployment](/wiki/Unemployment "Unemployment"), a strong tradition of [manufacturing](/wiki/Manufacturing "Manufacturing") employment, and in which the population had fewer [qualifications](/wiki/Qualification_types_in_the_United_Kingdom "Qualification types in the United Kingdom"). It also tended to be greater where there was a large flow of Eastern European migrants (mainly low-skilled workers) into areas with a large share of native low-skilled workers.[[67]](#cite_note-Becker-Fetzer-Novy-68) Those in lower [social grades](/wiki/NRS_social_grade "NRS social grade") (especially the [working class](/wiki/Working_class "Working class")) were more likely to vote Leave, while those in higher social grades (especially the [upper middle class](/wiki/Upper_middle_class "Upper middle class")) more likely to vote Remain.[[67]](#cite_note-Becker-Fetzer-Novy-68)[[68]](#cite_note-69)[[69]](#cite_note-Hobolt-70) Studies found that the Leave vote tended to be higher in areas affected by economic decline,[[70]](#cite_note-71) high rates of suicides and drug-related deaths,[[71]](#cite_note-72) and [austerity](/wiki/Austerity "Austerity") reforms introduced in 2010.[[72]](#cite_note-73)

Studies suggest that older people were more likely to vote Leave, and younger people more likely to vote Remain.[[73]](#cite_note-74) According to Thomas Sampson, an economist at the [London School of Economics](/wiki/London_School_of_Economics "London School of Economics"), "Older and less-educated voters were more likely to vote 'leave' [...] A majority of white voters wanted to leave, but only 33% of Asian voters and 27% of black voters chose leave. [...] Leaving the European Union received support from across the political spectrum [...] Voting to leave the European Union was strongly associated with holding socially conservative political beliefs, opposing cosmopolitanism, and thinking life in Britain is getting worse."[[74]](#cite_note-Sampson-2017-75)

Polling conducted by YouGov supported these conclusions, showing that factors such as age, political party affiliation, education, and household income were the primary factors indicating how people would vote. For example, [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") voters were 61% likely to vote leave, compared to Labour Party voters, who were 35% likely to vote leave. Age was one of the biggest factors affecting whether someone would vote leave, with 64% of people over the age of 65 likely to vote leave, whereas 18–24-year-olds were only 29% likely to vote leave. Education was another factor indicating voting likelihood: people with a [GCSE](/wiki/GCSE "GCSE") or lower level of education were 70% likely to vote leave, whereas university graduates were only 32% likely to vote leave. Household income was another important factor, with households earning less than £20,000 62% likely to vote leave, compared to households earning £60,000 or more, which were only 35% likely to vote leave.[[75]](#cite_note-76)

There were major variations in geographic support for each side. Scotland and Northern Ireland both returned majorities for remain, although these had a relatively small impact on the overall result as England has a much larger population. There were also significant regional differences within England, with most of London returning a majority remain vote, alongside urban centres in northern England such as [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester") and [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool "Liverpool"), which returned remain majorities of 60% and 58% respectively. Opposite trends appeared in industrial and [post-industrial](/wiki/Post-industrial_economy "Post-industrial economy") areas of [Northern England](/wiki/Northern_England "Northern England"), with areas such as [North Lincolnshire](/wiki/North_Lincolnshire "North Lincolnshire") and [South Tyneside](/wiki/South_Tyneside "South Tyneside") both heavily supporting leave.[[76]](#cite_note-77)

Opinion polls found that Leave voters believed leaving the EU was "more likely to bring about a better immigration system, improved border controls, a fairer welfare system, better quality of life, and the ability to control our own laws", while Remain voters believed EU membership "would be better for the economy, international investment, and the UK's influence in the world." Polls found that the main reasons people voted Leave were "the principle that decisions about the UK should be taken in the UK", and that leaving "offered the best chance for the UK to regain control over immigration and its own borders." The main reason people voted Remain was that "the risks of voting to leave the EU looked too great when it came to things like the economy, jobs and prices."[[77]](#cite_note-78)

### Post-referendum investigations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Post-referendum investigations")]

See also: [European Union membership referendum § Investigations into campaigns](/wiki/2016_United_Kingdom_European_Union_membership_referendum#Investigations_into_campaigns "2016 United Kingdom European Union membership referendum"), and [Allegations of unlawful campaigning in the 2016 EU referendum](/wiki/Allegations_of_unlawful_campaigning_in_the_2016_EU_referendum "Allegations of unlawful campaigning in the 2016 EU referendum")

Following the referendum, a series of irregularities related to campaign spending were investigated by the [Electoral Commission](/wiki/Electoral_Commission_(United_Kingdom) "Electoral Commission (United Kingdom)"), which subsequently issued a large number of fines. In February 2017, the main campaign group for the "Leave" vote, [Leave.EU](/wiki/Leave.EU "Leave.EU"), was fined £50,000 for sending marketing messages without permission.[[78]](#cite_note-79) In December 2017, the [Electoral Commission](/wiki/Electoral_Commission_(United_Kingdom) "Electoral Commission (United Kingdom)") fined two pro-EU groups, the [Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)") (£18,000) and [Open Britain](/wiki/Open_Britain "Open Britain") (£1,250), for breaches of campaign finance rules during the referendum campaign.[[79]](#cite_note-80) In May 2018, the Electoral Commission fined Leave.EU £70,000 for unlawfully overspending and inaccurately reporting loans from [Arron Banks](/wiki/Arron_Banks "Arron Banks") totalling £6 million.[[80]](#cite_note-81) Smaller fines were levelled against the pro-EU campaign group Best for Our Future and two trade union donors for inaccurate reporting.[[81]](#cite_note-82) In July 2018 [Vote Leave](/wiki/Vote_Leave "Vote Leave") was fined £61,000 for overspending, not declaring finances shared with [BeLeave](/wiki/BeLeave "BeLeave"), and failing to comply with investigators.[[82]](#cite_note-83)

In November 2017, the Electoral Commission launched a probe into [claims that Russia had attempted to sway public opinion](/wiki/Russian_interference_in_the_2016_Brexit_referendum "Russian interference in the 2016 Brexit referendum") over the referendum using social media platforms such as Twitter and Facebook.[[83]](#cite_note-84)

In February 2019, the parliamentary [Digital, Culture, Media and Sport Committee](/wiki/Digital,_Culture,_Media_and_Sport_Committee "Digital, Culture, Media and Sport Committee") called for an inquiry into "foreign influence, disinformation, funding, voter manipulation, and the sharing of data" in the Brexit vote.[[84]](#cite_note-85)

In July 2020, [Intelligence and Security Committee of Parliament](/wiki/Intelligence_and_Security_Committee_of_Parliament "Intelligence and Security Committee of Parliament") published a [report](/wiki/Intelligence_and_Security_Committee_Russia_report "Intelligence and Security Committee Russia report") which accused the UK government of actively avoiding investigating whether Russia interfered with public opinion. The report did not pass judgement over whether Russian information operations had an impact on the result.[[85]](#cite_note-86)

## Withdrawal process

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Withdrawal process")]

Further information: [Withdrawal from the European Union](/wiki/Withdrawal_from_the_European_Union "Withdrawal from the European Union")

[![logo](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Wikisource-logo.svg/40px-Wikisource-logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Wikisource-logo.svg)

English [Wikisource](/wiki/Wikisource "Wikisource") has original text related to this article:

**[Article 50 of the Treaty on European Union](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/en:Consolidated_version_of_the_Treaty_on_European_Union/Title_VI:_Final_Provisions#Article_50 "s:en:Consolidated version of the Treaty on European Union/Title VI: Final Provisions")**

Withdrawal from the European Union is governed by Article 50 of the [Treaty on European Union](/wiki/Treaty_on_European_Union "Treaty on European Union"). It was originally drafted by [Lord Kerr of Kinlochard](/wiki/Lord_Kerr_of_Kinlochard "Lord Kerr of Kinlochard"),[[86]](#cite_note-87) and introduced by the [Treaty of Lisbon](/wiki/Treaty_of_Lisbon "Treaty of Lisbon") which entered into force in 2009.[[87]](#cite_note-88) The article states that any member state can withdraw "in accordance with its own constitutional requirements" by notifying the [European Council](/wiki/European_Council "European Council") of its intention to do so.[[88]](#cite_note-89) The notification triggers a two-year negotiation period, in which the EU must "negotiate and conclude an agreement with [the leaving] State, setting out the arrangements for its withdrawal, taking account of the framework for its future relationship with the [European] Union".[[89]](#cite_note-constunit-90) If no agreement is reached within the two years, the membership ends without an agreement, unless an extension is unanimously agreed among all EU states, including the withdrawing state.[[89]](#cite_note-constunit-90) On the EU side, the agreement needs to be ratified by [qualified majority in the European Council](/wiki/Voting_in_the_Council_of_the_European_Union "Voting in the Council of the European Union"), and by the European Parliament.[[89]](#cite_note-constunit-90)

### Invocation of Article 50

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Invocation of Article 50")]

Main article: [United Kingdom invocation of Article 50 of the Treaty on European Union](/wiki/United_Kingdom_invocation_of_Article_50_of_the_Treaty_on_European_Union "United Kingdom invocation of Article 50 of the Treaty on European Union")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Prime_Minister%E2%80%99s_letter_to_Donald_Tusk_triggering_Article_50.pdf/page1-250px-Prime_Minister%E2%80%99s_letter_to_Donald_Tusk_triggering_Article_50.pdf.jpg)](/wiki/File:Prime_Minister%E2%80%99s_letter_to_Donald_Tusk_triggering_Article_50.pdf)

Letter from Theresa May invoking Article 50

The 2015 Referendum Act did not expressly require Article 50 to be invoked,[[89]](#cite_note-constunit-90) but prior to the referendum, the British government said it would respect the result.[[90]](#cite_note-91) When Cameron resigned following the referendum, he said that it would be for the incoming prime minister to invoke Article 50.[[61]](#cite_note-BBC24forecast-62)[[91]](#cite_note-92) The new prime minister, [Theresa May](/wiki/Theresa_May "Theresa May"), said she would wait until 2017 to invoke the article, in order to prepare for the negotiations.[[92]](#cite_note-93) In October 2016, she said Britain would trigger Article 50 in March 2017,[[93]](#cite_note-94) and in December she gained the support of MPs for her timetable.[[94]](#cite_note-95)

In January 2017, the [Supreme Court of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_Kingdom "Supreme Court of the United Kingdom") ruled in [the Miller case](/wiki/R_(Miller_and_Dos_Santos)_v_Secretary_of_State_for_Exiting_the_European_Union "R (Miller and Dos Santos) v Secretary of State for Exiting the European Union") that government could only invoke Article 50 if authorised by an act of parliament to do so.[[95]](#cite_note-96) The government subsequently introduced a bill for that purpose, and it was passed into law on 16 March as the [European Union (Notification of Withdrawal) Act 2017](/wiki/European_Union_(Notification_of_Withdrawal)_Act_2017 "European Union (Notification of Withdrawal) Act 2017").[[96]](#cite_note-97) On 29 March, [Theresa May](/wiki/Theresa_May "Theresa May") triggered Article 50 when [Tim Barrow](/wiki/Tim_Barrow "Tim Barrow"), the British ambassador to the EU, delivered the invocation letter to European Council President [Donald Tusk](/wiki/Donald_Tusk "Donald Tusk"). This made 29 March 2019 the expected date that UK would leave EU.[[97]](#cite_note-98)[[98]](#cite_note-99)

### 2017 UK general election

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: 2017 UK general election")]

See also: [2017 United Kingdom general election](/wiki/2017_United_Kingdom_general_election "2017 United Kingdom general election")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/2017UKElectionMap.svg/500px-2017UKElectionMap.svg.png)](/wiki/File:2017UKElectionMap.svg)

A map presenting the results of the 2017 United Kingdom general election, by party of the MP elected from each constituency, with Conservatives in blue, Labour in red, and SNP in yellow

In April 2017, [Theresa May](/wiki/Theresa_May "Theresa May") called a [snap general election, held on 8 June](/wiki/2017_United_Kingdom_general_election "2017 United Kingdom general election"), in an attempt to "strengthen [her] hand" in the negotiations;[[99]](#cite_note-100) The Conservative Party, Labour and UKIP made manifesto pledges to implement the referendum, the Labour manifesto differing in its approach to Brexit negotiations, such as unilaterally offering permanent residence to EU immigrants.[[100]](#cite_note-101)[[101]](#cite_note-102)[[102]](#cite_note-103) The Liberal Democrat Party and the [Green Party](/wiki/Green_Party_of_England_and_Wales "Green Party of England and Wales") manifestos proposed a policy of remaining in the EU via a [second referendum](/wiki/Proposed_referendum_on_the_Brexit_withdrawal_agreement "Proposed referendum on the Brexit withdrawal agreement").[[103]](#cite_note-104)[[104]](#cite_note-105)[[105]](#cite_note-106) The Scottish National Party (SNP) manifesto proposed a policy of waiting for the outcome of the Brexit negotiations and then holding a referendum on [Scottish independence](/wiki/Scottish_independence "Scottish independence").[[106]](#cite_note-107)[[107]](#cite_note-108)

The result produced an unexpected [hung parliament](/wiki/Hung_parliament "Hung parliament"), the governing Conservatives gained votes and remained the largest party but nevertheless lost seats and their majority in the House of Commons. Labour gained significantly on votes and seats, retaining its position as the second-largest party. The Liberal Democrats gained six seats despite a slight decrease in vote share compared with 2015. The Green Party kept its single MP while also losing national vote share. Losing votes and seats were the SNP, which lost 21 MPs, and UKIP, which suffered a −10.8% swing and lost its only MP. The [Democratic Unionist Party](/wiki/Democratic_Unionist_Party "Democratic Unionist Party") (DUP) and [Sinn Féin](/wiki/Sinn_F%C3%A9in "Sinn Féin") also made gains in votes and seats.[[108]](#cite_note-109)

On 26 June 2017, Conservatives and the DUP reached a [confidence and supply](/wiki/Confidence_and_supply "Confidence and supply") agreement whereby the DUP would back the Conservatives in key votes in the House of Commons over the course of the parliament. The agreement included additional funding of £1 billion for Northern Ireland, highlighted mutual support for Brexit and national security, expressed commitment to the Good Friday Agreement, and indicated that policies such as the state pension triple lock and [Winter Fuel Payments](/wiki/Winter_Fuel_Payment "Winter Fuel Payment") would be maintained.[[109]](#cite_note-110)[[110]](#cite_note-111)

### UK–EU negotiations in 2017 and 2018

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: UK–EU negotiations in 2017 and 2018")]

Main article: [Brexit negotiations](/wiki/Brexit_negotiations "Brexit negotiations")

Prior to the negotiations, May said that the British government would not seek permanent [single market membership](/wiki/Membership_of_the_United_Kingdom_in_the_European_Economic_Area "Membership of the United Kingdom in the European Economic Area"), would end ECJ jurisdiction, seek a new trade agreement, end [free movement of people](/wiki/Free_movement_of_people "Free movement of people") and maintain the [Common Travel Area](/wiki/Common_Travel_Area "Common Travel Area") with [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland").[[111]](#cite_note-112) The EU had adopted its [negotiating directives](/wiki/European_Union_negotiating_directives_for_Brexit "European Union negotiating directives for Brexit") in May,[[112]](#cite_note-113) and appointed [Michel Barnier](/wiki/Michel_Barnier "Michel Barnier") as Chief Negotiator.[[113]](#cite_note-Guardian-uk-caves-114) The EU wished to perform the negotiations in two phases: first the UK would agree to a financial commitment and to lifelong benefits for EU citizens in Britain, and then negotiations on a future relationship could begin.[[114]](#cite_note-115) In the first phase, the member states would demand that the UK pay a "[divorce bill](/wiki/Brexit_divorce_bill "Brexit divorce bill")", initially estimated as amounting to £52 billion.[[115]](#cite_note-116) EU negotiators said that an agreement must be reached between UK and the EU by October 2018.[[116]](#cite_note-brexitoct18-117)

Negotiations commenced on 19 June 2017.[[113]](#cite_note-Guardian-uk-caves-114) Negotiating groups were established for three topics: the rights of EU citizens living in Britain and vice versa; Britain's outstanding financial obligations to the EU; and the border between Northern Ireland and the Republic of Ireland.[[117]](#cite_note-118)[[118]](#cite_note-119)[[119]](#cite_note-120) In December 2017, a partial agreement was reached. It ensured that there would be no hard border in Ireland, protected the rights of UK citizens in the EU and of EU citizens in Britain, and estimated the financial settlement to be £35–39 billion.[[120]](#cite_note-121) May stressed that "Nothing is agreed until everything is agreed".[[121]](#cite_note-122) Following this partial agreement, EU leaders agreed to move on to the second phase in the negotiations: discussion of the future relationship, a transition period and a possible trade deal.[[122]](#cite_note-123)

In March 2018, a 21-month transition period and the terms for it were provisionally agreed.[[123]](#cite_note-124) In June 2018, Irish [Taoiseach](/wiki/Taoiseach "Taoiseach") [Leo Varadkar](/wiki/Leo_Varadkar "Leo Varadkar") said that there had been little progress on the [Irish border question](/wiki/Irish_border_question "Irish border question")—on which the EU proposed a [backstop](/wiki/Irish_backstop "Irish backstop"), to come into effect if no overall trade deal had been reached by the end of the transition period—and that it was unlikely that there would be a solution before October, when the whole deal was to be agreed.[[124]](#cite_note-125) In July 2018, the British government published the [Chequers plan](/wiki/Chequers_plan "Chequers plan"), containing its aims for the future relationship that was to be determined in the negotiations. The plan sought to keep British access to the single market for goods, but not necessarily for services, while allowing for an independent [trade policy](/wiki/Trade_policy "Trade policy").[[125]](#cite_note-126) The plan caused cabinet resignations, including those of [Brexit Secretary](/wiki/Brexit_Secretary "Brexit Secretary") [David Davis](/wiki/David_Davis_(British_politician) "David Davis (British politician)")[[126]](#cite_note-127) and [Foreign Secretary](/wiki/Foreign_Secretary_(United_Kingdom) "Foreign Secretary (United Kingdom)") [Boris Johnson](/wiki/Boris_Johnson "Boris Johnson").[[127]](#cite_note-S,_C,_S-128)

### May's agreement and failed ratification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: May's agreement and failed ratification")]

Main articles: [Brexit withdrawal agreement](/wiki/Brexit_withdrawal_agreement "Brexit withdrawal agreement") and [Parliamentary votes on Brexit](/wiki/Parliamentary_votes_on_Brexit "Parliamentary votes on Brexit")

[![logo](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Wikisource-logo.svg/40px-Wikisource-logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Wikisource-logo.svg)

English [Wikisource](/wiki/Wikisource "Wikisource") has original text related to this article:

**[2018 Draft withdrawal agreement](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/en:Draft_Agreement_on_the_Withdrawal_of_the_United_Kingdom_from_the_European_Union "s:en:Draft Agreement on the Withdrawal of the United Kingdom from the European Union")**

On 13 November 2018, UK and EU negotiators agreed the text of a draft withdrawal agreement,[[128]](#cite_note-129) and May secured her Cabinet's backing of the deal the following day,[[129]](#cite_note-130) though Brexit Secretary [Dominic Raab](/wiki/Dominic_Raab "Dominic Raab") resigned over "fatal flaws" in the agreement.[[130]](#cite_note-131) It was expected that ratification in the British parliament would be difficult.[[131]](#cite_note-BBC_News-2019-132)[[132]](#cite_note-Cook-2018-133)[[133]](#cite_note-134) On 25 November, all 27 leaders of the remaining EU countries endorsed the agreement.[[131]](#cite_note-BBC_News-2019-132)[[132]](#cite_note-Cook-2018-133)

On 10 December 2018, the Prime Minister postponed the vote in the House of Commons on her Brexit deal. This came minutes after the [Prime Minister's Office](/wiki/Prime_Minister%27s_Office_(United_Kingdom) "Prime Minister's Office (United Kingdom)") confirmed the vote would be going ahead.[[134]](#cite_note-135) Faced with the prospect of a defeat in the House of Commons, this option gave May more time to negotiate with Conservative [backbenchers](/wiki/Backbencher "Backbencher") and the EU, even though they had ruled out further discussions.[[135]](#cite_note-136) The decision was met with calls from many [Welsh Labour](/wiki/Welsh_Labour "Welsh Labour") MPs for a [motion of no confidence](/wiki/Motions_of_no_confidence_in_the_United_Kingdom "Motions of no confidence in the United Kingdom") in the Government.[[136]](#cite_note-137)

Also on 10 December 2018, the [European Court of Justice](/wiki/European_Court_of_Justice "European Court of Justice") (ECJ) ruled that the UK could unilaterally revoke its notification of withdrawal, as long as it was still a member and had not agreed a withdrawal agreement. The decision to do so should be "unequivocal and unconditional" and "follow a democratic process".[[137]](#cite_note-138) If the British revoked their notification, they would remain a member of the EU under their current membership terms. The case was launched by Scottish politicians and referred to the ECJ by the Scottish [Court of Session](/wiki/Court_of_Session "Court of Session").[[138]](#cite_note-139)

The [European Research Group](/wiki/European_Research_Group "European Research Group") (ERG), a research support group of Eurosceptic Conservative MPs, opposed the Prime Minister's proposed Withdrawal Agreement treaty. Its members objected strongly to the Withdrawal Agreement's inclusion of the [Irish backstop](/wiki/Irish_backstop "Irish backstop").[[139]](#cite_note-140)[[140]](#cite_note-141) ERG members also objected to the proposed £39 billion financial settlement with the EU and stated that the agreement would result in the UK's agreement to continuing to follow EU regulations in major policy areas; and to the continuing jurisdiction of the ECJ over interpretation of the agreement and of European law still applicable to the UK.[[141]](#cite_note-142)[[142]](#cite_note-143)

On 15 January 2019, the House of Commons voted 432 to 202 against the deal, which was the largest majority ever against a United Kingdom government.[[143]](#cite_note-Brexit:_Theresa_May's_deal_is_voted_down_in_historic_Commons_defeat-144)[[144]](#cite_note-145) Soon after, [a motion of no confidence in Her Majesty's Government was tabled by the opposition](/wiki/2019_motion_of_no_confidence_in_the_May_ministry "2019 motion of no confidence in the May ministry"),[[145]](#cite_note-146) which was rejected by 325 votes to 306.[[146]](#cite_note-147)

On 24 February, Prime Minister May proposed that the next vote on the withdrawal agreement would be on 12 March 2019, 17 days away from the Brexit date.[[147]](#cite_note-148) On 12 March, the proposal was defeated by 391 votes to 242—a loss by 149 votes, down from 230 from when the deal had been proposed in January.[[148]](#cite_note-149)

On 18 March 2019, the [Speaker](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_House_of_Commons_(United_Kingdom) "Speaker of the House of Commons (United Kingdom)") informed the House of Commons that a third meaningful vote could be held only on a [motion](/wiki/Motion_(parliamentary_procedure) "Motion (parliamentary procedure)") that was significantly different from the previous one, citing parliamentary precedents going back to 1604.[[149]](#cite_note-150)

The Withdrawal Agreement was brought back to the House without the attached understandings on 29 March.[[150]](#cite_note-151) The Government's motion of support for the Withdrawal Agreement was defeated by 344 votes to 286—a loss by 58 votes, down from 149 when the deal had been proposed on 12 March.[[151]](#cite_note-152)

### Article 50 extensions and Johnson's agreement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Article 50 extensions and Johnson's agreement")]

On 20 March 2019, the Prime Minister wrote to European Council President Tusk requesting that Brexit be postponed until 30 June 2019.[[152]](#cite_note-153) On 21 March 2019, May presented her case to a European Council summit meeting in Brussels. After May left the meeting, a discussion amongst the remaining EU leaders resulted in the rejection of 30 June date and offered instead a choice of two new alternative Brexit dates. On 22 March 2019, the extension options were agreed between the British government and the European Council.[[153]](#cite_note-SI2019-03-28a-154) The first alternative offered was that if MPs rejected May's deal in the next week, Brexit would be due to occur by 12 April 2019, with, or without, a deal—or alternatively another extension be asked for and a commitment to participate in the [2019 European Parliament elections](/wiki/2019_European_Parliament_election "2019 European Parliament election") given. The second alternative offered was that if MPs approved May's deal, Brexit would be due to occur on 22 May 2019. The later date was the day before the start of European Parliament elections.[[154]](#cite_note-BBC_20190322-155) After the government deemed unwarranted the concerns over the legality of the proposed change (because it contained two possible exit dates) the previous day,[[155]](#cite_note-TWFY2019-03-26a-156) on 27 March 2019 both the Lords (without a vote)[[156]](#cite_note-Guardian2019-03-27a-157) and the Commons (by 441 to 105) approved the statutory instrument changing the exit date to 22 May 2019 if a withdrawal deal is approved, or 12 April 2019 if it is not.[[157]](#cite_note-FT2019-03-27a-158) The amendment was then signed into law at 12:40 p.m. the next day.[[153]](#cite_note-SI2019-03-28a-154)

Following the failure of the British Parliament to approve the Withdrawal Agreement by 29 March, the UK was required to leave the EU on 12 April 2019. On 10 April 2019, late-night talks in Brussels resulted in a further extension, to 31 October 2019; [Theresa May](/wiki/Theresa_May "Theresa May") had again requested an extension only until 30 June. Under the terms of this new extension, if the Withdrawal Agreement were to be passed before October, Brexit would occur on the first day of the subsequent month. The UK would then be obligated to hold European Parliament elections in May or leave the EU on 1 June without a deal.[[158]](#cite_note-159)[[159]](#cite_note-160)

In granting the Article 50 extensions, the EU adopted a stance of refusing to "reopen" (that is, renegotiate) the Withdrawal Agreement.[[160]](#cite_note-161) After [Boris Johnson](/wiki/Boris_Johnson "Boris Johnson") became prime minister on 24 July 2019 and met with EU leaders, the EU changed its stance. On 17 October 2019, following "tunnel talks" between UK and EU,[[161]](#cite_note-162) a revised withdrawal agreement was agreed on negotiators level, and endorsed by the British government and the EU Commission.[[162]](#cite_note-163) The revised deal contained a new [Northern Ireland Protocol](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Protocol "Northern Ireland Protocol"), as well as technical modifications to related articles.[[8]](#cite_note-Europa17oct19-9) In addition, the Political Declaration was also revised.[[163]](#cite_note-164) The revised deal and the political declaration was endorsed by the [European Council](/wiki/European_Council "European Council") later that day.[[164]](#cite_note-165) To come into effect, it needed to be ratified by the [European Parliament](/wiki/European_Parliament "European Parliament") and the [Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom").[[165]](#cite_note-166)

The British Parliament passed the [European Union (Withdrawal) (No. 2) Act 2019](/wiki/European_Union_(Withdrawal)_(No._2)_Act_2019 "European Union (Withdrawal) (No. 2) Act 2019"), which received [Royal Assent](/wiki/Royal_Assent "Royal Assent") on 9 September 2019, obliging the Prime Minister to seek a third extension if no agreement has been reached at the next European Council meeting in October 2019.[[166]](#cite_note-167) In order for such an extension to be granted if it is requested by the prime minister, it would be necessary for there to be unanimous agreement by all other heads of EU governments.[[167]](#cite_note-168) On 28 October 2019, the third extension was agreed to by the EU, with a new withdrawal deadline of 31 January 2020.[[168]](#cite_note-169) 'Exit day' in British law was then amended to this new date by statutory instrument on 30 October 2019.[[169]](#cite_note-SI2019-10-30a-170)

### 2019 UK general election

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: 2019 UK general election")]

See also: [2019 United Kingdom general election](/wiki/2019_United_Kingdom_general_election "2019 United Kingdom general election")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/2019UKElectionMap.svg/500px-2019UKElectionMap.svg.png)](/wiki/File:2019UKElectionMap.svg)

Map for results of the [2019 general election in the United Kingdom](/wiki/2019_general_election_in_the_United_Kingdom "2019 general election in the United Kingdom"). Ultimately based upon data from the Boundary Commission. Colours are as below.

[Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)")

[Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)")

[Scottish National Party](/wiki/Scottish_National_Party "Scottish National Party")

[Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)")

[Democratic Unionist Party](/wiki/Democratic_Unionist_Party "Democratic Unionist Party")

[Sinn Féin](/wiki/Sinn_F%C3%A9in "Sinn Féin")

[Social Democratic and Labour Party](/wiki/SDLP "SDLP")

[Plaid Cymru](/wiki/Plaid_Cymru "Plaid Cymru")

After Johnson was unable to induce Parliament to approve a revised version of the [withdrawal agreement](/wiki/Withdrawal_agreement "Withdrawal agreement") by the end of October, he chose to call for a [snap election](/wiki/Snap_election "Snap election"). Due to the fact three motions for an early general election under the [Fixed-term Parliaments Act 2011](/wiki/Fixed-term_Parliaments_Act_2011 "Fixed-term Parliaments Act 2011") failed to achieve the necessary two-thirds super majority for it to pass so instead, in order to circumvent the existing law, the Government introduced an "[election bill](/wiki/Early_Parliamentary_General_Election_Act_2019 "Early Parliamentary General Election Act 2019")" which only needed a simple majority of MPs to vote in favour into the House of Commons which was passed by 438–20, setting the election date for Thursday 12 December.[[170]](#cite_note-171) [Opinion polls](/wiki/Opinion_polling_for_the_2019_United_Kingdom_general_election "Opinion polling for the 2019 United Kingdom general election") up to polling day showed a firm lead for the Conservatives against Labour throughout the campaign.[[171]](#cite_note-172)

In the run-up to the general election on 12 December 2019 the Conservative Party pledged to leave the EU with the withdrawal agreement negotiated in October 2019. Labour promised to renegotiate aforementioned deal and hold a referendum, letting voters choose between the renegotiated deal and remain. The Liberal Democrats vowed to revoke Article 50, while the SNP intended to hold a second referendum, however, revoking Article 50 if the alternative was a no-deal exit. The DUP supported Brexit but would seek to change parts related to Northern Ireland it was dissatisfied with. [Plaid Cymru](/wiki/Plaid_Cymru "Plaid Cymru") and the [Green Party](/wiki/Green_Party_of_England_and_Wales "Green Party of England and Wales") backed a second referendum, believing the UK should stay in the EU. The [Brexit Party](/wiki/Brexit_Party "Brexit Party") was the only major party running for election which wanted the UK to leave the EU without a deal.[[172]](#cite_note-173)

The election produced a decisive result for Boris Johnson with the Conservatives winning 365 seats (gaining 47 seats) and an overall majority of 80 seats with Labour suffering their worst election defeat since 1935 after losing 60 seats to leave them with 202 seats and only a single seat in [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"). The Liberal Democrats won just 11 seats with their leader [Jo Swinson](/wiki/Jo_Swinson "Jo Swinson") losing her own seat. The Scottish National Party won 48 seats after gaining 14 seats in Scotland.

The result broke the [deadlock in the UK Parliament](/wiki/Parliamentary_votes_on_Brexit "Parliamentary votes on Brexit") and ended the possibility of a [referendum being held on the withdrawal agreement](/wiki/Proposed_referendum_on_the_Brexit_withdrawal_agreement "Proposed referendum on the Brexit withdrawal agreement") and ensured that the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") would leave the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") on 31 January 2020.

### Ratification and departure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Ratification and departure")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Westminster_%2849470617471%29.jpg/250px-Westminster_%2849470617471%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Westminster_(49470617471).jpg)

[Foreign and Commonwealth Office](/wiki/Foreign_and_Commonwealth_Office "Foreign and Commonwealth Office") illuminated in the colours of the [Union Jack](/wiki/Union_Jack "Union Jack") on 31 January 2020

Subsequently, the government introduced a bill to ratify the withdrawal agreement. It passed its second reading in the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") in a 358–234 vote on 20 December 2019,[[173]](#cite_note-174) and became law on 23 January 2020 as the [European Union (Withdrawal Agreement) Act 2020](/wiki/European_Union_(Withdrawal_Agreement)_Act_2020 "European Union (Withdrawal Agreement) Act 2020").[[174]](#cite_note-175)

The withdrawal agreement received the backing of the [constitutional committee](/wiki/European_Parliament_Committee_on_Constitutional_Affairs "European Parliament Committee on Constitutional Affairs") in the [European Parliament](/wiki/European_Parliament "European Parliament") on 23 January 2020, setting expectation that the entire parliament would approve it in a later vote.[[175]](#cite_note-176)[[176]](#cite_note-177)[[177]](#cite_note-178) On the following day, [Ursula von der Leyen](/wiki/Ursula_von_der_Leyen "Ursula von der Leyen") and [Charles Michel](/wiki/Charles_Michel "Charles Michel") signed the withdrawal agreement in Brussels, and it was sent to London where [Boris Johnson](/wiki/Boris_Johnson "Boris Johnson") signed it.[[11]](#cite_note-Guardian2020jan24-12) The European Parliament gave its consent to ratification on 29 January by 621 votes to 49.[[178]](#cite_note-179)[[12]](#cite_note-sparrow1-13) Immediately after voting approval, members of the European Parliament joined hands and sang *[Auld Lang Syne](/wiki/Auld_Lang_Syne "Auld Lang Syne")*.[[179]](#cite_note-180) The [Council of the European Union](/wiki/Council_of_the_European_Union "Council of the European Union") concluded EU ratification the following day.[[180]](#cite_note-181) At 11 p.m. [GMT](/wiki/GMT "GMT"), 31 January 2020, the United Kingdom's membership of the European Union ended, 47 years after it had joined.[[13]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-14) As confirmed by the [Court of Justice](/wiki/Court_of_Justice_of_the_European_Union "Court of Justice of the European Union") in *EP v Préfet du Gers*,[[181]](#cite_note-182) all British nationals ceased to be Union citizens.[[182]](#cite_note-183)

To commemorate the moment of Brexit, a countdown clock was projected onto 10 Downing Street with a recording of Big Ben chiming. In addition, there was a nearby party in [Parliament Square](/wiki/Parliament_Square "Parliament Square"), being led by Farage, sang "[God Save the Queen](/wiki/God_Save_the_Queen "God Save the Queen")" at the moment of departure.[[183]](#cite_note-184) In Gibraltar, a flag ceremony was held as the EU flag was lowered to "[Ode to Joy](/wiki/Ode_to_Joy "Ode to Joy")" and the [Commonwealth flag](/wiki/Commonwealth_flag "Commonwealth flag") was raised to "God Save the Queen".[[184]](#cite_note-185)

### Transition period and final trade agreement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Transition period and final trade agreement")]

Conservative party advertisement from early 2020 featuring [Boris Johnson](/wiki/Boris_Johnson "Boris Johnson") answering frequently searched for online Brexit-related questions

Following the British exit on 31 January 2020 the UK entered a Transition Period for the rest of 2020. Trade, travel and freedom of movement remain largely unchanged during this period.[[185]](#cite_note-transition-186)

The Withdrawal Agreement still applies after this date.[[186]](#cite_note-eu-sues-wa-187) This agreement [provides](/wiki/Brexit_withdrawal_agreement#Northern_Ireland "Brexit withdrawal agreement") free access of goods between Northern Ireland and the Republic of Ireland, provided checks are made to goods entering Northern Ireland from the rest of the UK.
