The British Government attempted to back out of this commitment[[187]](#cite_note-specific-188) by passing the [Internal Market Bill](/wiki/Internal_Market_Bill "Internal Market Bill"): domestic legislation in the British Parliament. In September, Northern Ireland secretary [Brandon Lewis](/wiki/Brandon_Lewis "Brandon Lewis") said:

> I would say to my hon. Friend that yes, this does break international law in a very specific and limited way.[[188]](#cite_note-189)

leading to the resignation of [Sir Jonathan Jones](/wiki/Jonathan_Jones_(civil_servant) "Jonathan Jones (civil servant)"), permanent secretary to the Government Legal Department[[189]](#cite_note-190) and [Lord Keen](/wiki/Lord_Keen "Lord Keen"), the law officer for Scotland.[[190]](#cite_note-191) The [European Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission") started legal action.[[186]](#cite_note-eu-sues-wa-187)

During the transition period, David Frost and Michel Barnier continued to [negotiate a permanent trade agreement](/wiki/Trade_negotiation_between_the_UK_and_the_EU "Trade negotiation between the UK and the EU").[[191]](#cite_note-192) On 24 December 2020 both parties announced that a deal had been reached.[[192]](#cite_note-193) The deal was passed by both houses of the British parliament on 30 December and given Royal Assent in the early hours of the next day. In the House of Commons, the governing Conservatives and main opposition Labour voted in favour of the agreement whilst all other opposition parties voted against it.[[193]](#cite_note-194) The transition period concluded under its terms the following evening.[[194]](#cite_note-195) After the UK said it would unilaterally extend a [grace period](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Protocol#Grace_period "Northern Ireland Protocol") limiting checks on trade between Northern Ireland and Great Britain, the [European Parliament](/wiki/European_Parliament "European Parliament") postponed setting a date to ratify the agreement.[[195]](#cite_note-196) The vote was later scheduled for 27 April when it passed with an overwhelming majority of votes.[[196]](#cite_note-197)[[197]](#cite_note-198)

There was a customs transitional arrangement in place until 1 July 2021. During this time period, traders importing standard goods from the EU to the UK could defer submitting their customs declarations and paying import duties to HMRC for up to six months. This arrangement simplified and avoided most import controls during the early months of the new situation and was designed to facilitate inward trade during the COVID-19 health crisis and to avoid major disruptions in domestic supply chains in the short term.[[198]](#cite_note-199) Following reports that the border infrastructure was not ready, the UK government further postponed import checks from the EU to the UK until the end of the year in order to avoid supply issues during the ongoing Covid crisis.[[199]](#cite_note-200) This was again followed by another delay of import controls, in a situation of truck driver shortages; the controls are scheduled to be phased in during 2022.[[200]](#cite_note-201)

## United Kingdom legislation after Article 50 notification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: United Kingdom legislation after Article 50 notification")]

### European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018")]

Main article: [European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018](/wiki/European_Union_(Withdrawal)_Act_2018 "European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018")

In October 2016, Theresa May promised a "Great Repeal Bill", which would repeal the [European Communities Act 1972](/wiki/European_Communities_Act_1972_(UK) "European Communities Act 1972 (UK)") and restate in British law all enactments previously in force under EU law. Subsequently renamed the European Union (Withdrawal) Bill, it was introduced into the House of Commons on 13 July 2017.[[201]](#cite_note-202)

On 12 September 2017, the Bill passed its first vote and second reading by a margin of 326 votes to 290 votes in the House of Commons.[[202]](#cite_note-203) The Bill was further amended on a series of votes in both Houses. After the Act became law on 26 June 2018, the European Council decided on 29 June to renew its call on member states and European Union institutions to step up their work on preparedness at all levels and for all outcomes.[[203]](#cite_note-204)

The Withdrawal Act fixed the period ending 21 January 2019 for the government to decide on how to proceed if the negotiations had not reached agreement in principle on both the withdrawal arrangements and the framework for the future relationship between the UK and EU; while, alternatively, making future ratification of the withdrawal agreement as a treaty between the UK and EU depend upon the prior enactment of another act of Parliament for approving the final terms of withdrawal when the [Brexit negotiations](/wiki/Brexit_negotiations "Brexit negotiations") were completed. In any event, the Act did not alter the two-year period for negotiating allowed by Article 50 that ended at the latest on 29 March 2019 if the UK had not by then ratified a withdrawal agreement or agreed a prolongation of the negotiating period.[[204]](#cite_note-ExitDay01a-205)

The Withdrawal Act which became law in June 2018 allowed for various outcomes including no negotiated settlement. It authorises the government to bring into force, by [order](/wiki/Statutory_instrument_(UK) "Statutory instrument (UK)") made under section 25, the provisions that fixed "exit day" and the repeal of the European Communities Act 1972 but exit day must be the same day and time as when the EU Treaties ceased to apply to the UK.[[205]](#cite_note-206)

### Exit day

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Exit day")]

Exit day was the end of 31 January 2020 [CET](/wiki/Central_European_Time "Central European Time") (11.00 p.m. [GMT](/wiki/GMT "GMT")).[[169]](#cite_note-SI2019-10-30a-170) The [European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018](/wiki/European_Union_(Withdrawal)_Act_2018 "European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018") (as amended by a British [Statutory Instrument](/wiki/Statutory_Instrument "Statutory Instrument") on 11 April 2019), in section 20 (1), defined 'exit day' as 11:00 p.m. on 31 October 2019.[[153]](#cite_note-SI2019-03-28a-154) Originally, 'exit day' was defined as 11:00 p.m. on 29 March 2019 GMT ([UTC+0](/wiki/UTC%2B0 "UTC+0")).[[204]](#cite_note-ExitDay01a-205)[[206]](#cite_note-reuters1-207)[[207]](#cite_note-208)

### Additional government bills

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Additional government bills")]

A report published in March 2017 by the [Institute for Government](/wiki/Institute_for_Government "Institute for Government") commented that, in addition to the European Union (Withdrawal) bill, primary and secondary legislation would be needed to cover the gaps in policy areas such as customs, immigration and agriculture.[[208]](#cite_note-209) The report also commented that the role of the devolved legislatures was unclear, and could cause problems, and that as many as 15 new additional Brexit Bills might be required, which would involve strict prioritisation and limiting Parliamentary time for in-depth examination of new legislation.[[209]](#cite_note-210)

In 2016 and 2017, the House of Lords published a series of reports on Brexit-related subjects, including:

* [Brexit: the options for trade](https://www.publications.parliament.uk/pa/ld201617/ldselect/ldeucom/72/72.pdf)
* [Brexit: UK–Irish relations](https://www.publications.parliament.uk/pa/ld201617/ldselect/ldeucom/76/76.pdf)
* [Brexit: future UK–EU security and police cooperation](https://www.publications.parliament.uk/pa/ld201617/ldselect/ldeucom/77/77.pdf)
* [Brexit: fisheries](https://www.publications.parliament.uk/pa/ld201617/ldselect/ldeucom/78/78.pdf)
* [Brexit: environment and climate change](https://www.publications.parliament.uk/pa/ld201617/ldselect/ldeucom/109/109.pdf)
* [Brexit: the Crown Dependencies](https://www.publications.parliament.uk/pa/ld201617/ldselect/ldeucom/136/136.pdf)
* [Brexit: justice for families, individuals and businesses?](https://www.publications.parliament.uk/pa/ld201617/ldselect/ldeucom/134/134.pdf)
* [Brexit: trade in non-financial services](https://www.publications.parliament.uk/pa/ld201617/ldselect/ldeucom/135/135.pdf)

#### Nuclear Safeguards Act 2018

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Nuclear Safeguards Act 2018")]

The [Nuclear Safeguards Act 2018](/wiki/Nuclear_Safeguards_Act_2018 "Nuclear Safeguards Act 2018"), relating to withdrawal from Euratom, was presented to Parliament in October 2017. The act makes provision about nuclear safeguards, and for connected purposes. The Secretary of State may by regulations ("nuclear safeguards regulations") make provision for the purpose of – (a) ensuring that qualifying nuclear material, facilities or equipment are available only for use for civil activities (whether in the UK or elsewhere), or (b) giving effect to provisions of a relevant international agreement.[[210]](#cite_note-211)

#### European Union (Withdrawal Agreement) Act 2020

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: European Union (Withdrawal Agreement) Act 2020")]

Main article: [European Union (Withdrawal Agreement) Act 2020](/wiki/European_Union_(Withdrawal_Agreement)_Act_2020 "European Union (Withdrawal Agreement) Act 2020")

The [European Union (Withdrawal Agreement) Act 2020](/wiki/European_Union_(Withdrawal_Agreement)_Act_2020 "European Union (Withdrawal Agreement) Act 2020") makes legal provision for ratifying the [Brexit Withdrawal Agreement](/wiki/Brexit_Withdrawal_Agreement "Brexit Withdrawal Agreement") and incorporating it into the domestic law of the United Kingdom.[[211]](#cite_note-212) The bill was first introduced[[212]](#cite_note-213) by the government on 21 October 2019. This bill was not further debated and lapsed on 6 November when [parliament was dissolved](/wiki/Dissolution_of_parliament "Dissolution of parliament") in preparation for the [2019 general election](/wiki/2019_United_Kingdom_general_election "2019 United Kingdom general election"). The bill was reintroduced immediately following the general election and was the first bill to be put before the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") in the first session of the 58th Parliament,[[213]](#cite_note-214) with changes from the previous bill, by the re-elected government and was [read a first time](/wiki/Reading_(legislature) "Reading (legislature)") on 19 December, immediately after the first reading of the [Outlawries Bill](/wiki/Outlawries_Bill "Outlawries Bill") and before the debate on the [Queen's Speech](/wiki/State_Opening_of_Parliament "State Opening of Parliament") began. The second reading took place on 20 December, and the third on 9 January 2020. This act was given [Royal Assent](/wiki/Royal_Assent "Royal Assent") on 23 January 2020, nine days before the UK left the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union").

## Public opinion since the Brexit referendum

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Public opinion since the Brexit referendum")]

### Prior to 2020

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Prior to 2020")]

Further information: [Opinion polling on the United Kingdom's membership of the European Union (2016–2020)](/wiki/Opinion_polling_on_the_United_Kingdom%27s_membership_of_the_European_Union_(2016%E2%80%932020) "Opinion polling on the United Kingdom's membership of the European Union (2016–2020)")

Opinion polling overall showed an initial fall in support for Brexit from the referendum to late 2016, when responses were split evenly between support and opposition. Support rose again to a plurality, which held until the [2017 general election](/wiki/2017_United_Kingdom_general_election "2017 United Kingdom general election"). Since then, opinion polls tended to show a plurality of support for remaining in the EU or for the view that Brexit was a mistake, with the estimated margin increasing until a small decrease in 2019 (to 53% Remain: 47% Leave, as of October 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit)).[[214]](#cite_note-OctoberPollOfPolls-215) This seems to be largely due to a preference for remaining in the EU among those who did not vote in 2016's referendum (an estimated 2.5 million of whom, as of October 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit), were [too young to vote](/wiki/Voting_age#United_Kingdom "Voting age") at the time).[[215]](#cite_note-IndependentDemographics-216)[[216]](#cite_note-217) Other reasons suggested include slightly more Leave voters than Remain voters (14% and 12% of each, respectively, as of October 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit))[[217]](#cite_note-218) changing how they would vote (particularly in [Labour](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") areas) and the deaths of older voters,[[214]](#cite_note-OctoberPollOfPolls-215) most of whom voted to leave the EU. One estimate of demographic changes (ignoring other effects) implies that had an EU referendum taken place in October 2019,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit) there would have been between 800,000 and 900,000 fewer Leave voters and between 600,000 and 700,000 more Remain voters, resulting in a Remain majority.[[215]](#cite_note-IndependentDemographics-216)

In March 2019, a [petition](/wiki/Revoke_Article_50_and_remain_in_the_EU_petition "Revoke Article 50 and remain in the EU petition") submitted to the British Parliament petitions website, calling on the government to revoke Article 50 and stay in the EU, reached a record-level of more than 6.1 million signatures.[[218]](#cite_note-219)[[219]](#cite_note-220)

* Post-referendum opinion polling (2016–2020)
* [![Opinion polling on whether the UK was right or wrong to vote to leave the EU](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Brexit_post-referendum_polling_-_Right-Wrong.svg/500px-Brexit_post-referendum_polling_-_Right-Wrong.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Brexit_post-referendum_polling_-_Right-Wrong.svg "Opinion polling on whether the UK was right or wrong to vote to leave the EU")

  Opinion polling on whether the UK was right or wrong to vote to leave the EU
* [![Opinion polling on whether the UK should leave or remain in the EU, including "Neither" responses](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Brexit_post-referendum_polling_-_Remain-Leave-Neither.svg/500px-Brexit_post-referendum_polling_-_Remain-Leave-Neither.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Brexit_post-referendum_polling_-_Remain-Leave-Neither.svg "Opinion polling on whether the UK should leave or remain in the EU, including \"Neither\" responses")

  Opinion polling on whether the UK should leave or remain in the EU, including "Neither" responses
* [![Opinion polling on whether the UK should leave or remain in the EU, excluding "Neither" responses and normalised](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Brexit_post-referendum_polling_-_Remain-Leave.svg/500px-Brexit_post-referendum_polling_-_Remain-Leave.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Brexit_post-referendum_polling_-_Remain-Leave.svg "Opinion polling on whether the UK should leave or remain in the EU, excluding \"Neither\" responses and normalised")

  Opinion polling on whether the UK should leave or remain in the EU, excluding "Neither" responses and normalised

### 2020–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: 2020–present")]

Further information: [Opinion polling on the United Kingdom rejoining the European Union (2020–present)](/wiki/Opinion_polling_on_the_United_Kingdom_rejoining_the_European_Union_(2020%E2%80%93present) "Opinion polling on the United Kingdom rejoining the European Union (2020–present)")

[YouGov](/wiki/YouGov "YouGov") polling has shown a gradual but progressive decline in the public perception of the benefits of Brexit, with the overall margin of sentiment about the rightness of the Brexit decision declining from slightly positive in 2016 to −11% in 2022.[[220]](#cite_note-221) A May 2022 poll showed that a majority of respondents who expressed an opinion thought that Brexit had gone either "badly" or "very badly".[[221]](#cite_note-222) A new study showed that since Brexit, citizens in other European nations were more against leaving the EU than they had been since 2016.[[222]](#cite_note-223) A January 2023 poll in the United Kingdom also reflected these numbers, with 54% of poll respondents who believed that the country was wrong to leave the European Union, while 35% of respondents believed it was the right decision.[[223]](#cite_note-224) An average of six polls conducted in June and July 2023 shows 58% of voters in favour of rejoining the EU with 42% of voters against rejoining the EU.[[224]](#cite_note-225)

Since 2020, pollsters have asked respondents how they would vote in a potential second referendum to rejoin the EU.

As shown in the chart below, five and a half years after the United Kingdom left the European Union, opinions about Brexit and the option to rejoin the EU had changed significantly. After the first polls data was released shortly after the UK left the EU, both support for rejoining it or staying out of it declined over the course of 2020, with a noticeable increase of neutral opinions. However, after one year and a half, poll results were showing more support for rejoining EU than staying out of it, and rejoin support consistently increased over the next three years, going from below 40% in the spring of 2020 to surpassing Brexit support between August and September 2021 and rising to slightly below 50% in early 2024. On the other hand, support for staying out of the EU greatly dropped in 2022, falling below 40% in spring and never reaching this threshold again, and by summer 2023, pro-Brexit support reached its nadir, with only a third of votes in polls data supporting it. While the gap was reduced over the course of 2024, it became even wider in the first half of 2025 due to a second, sharper rise of popular support in favour of the return in the EU, while support in favour of staying out of it kept remaining poor, once again falling below the 35% threshold in the middle of the year. In between May and June 2025, pro-rejoin-EU support in polls data reached the 50% supermajority threshold and surpassed it in July.

In autumn 2025, UK Chancellor Rachel Reeves said the economic damage caused by Brexit had forced her to take action, including tax rises and spending cuts in the 2025 budget.[[225]](#cite_note-226)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Opinion_polling_on_the_whether_the_United_Kingdom_should_rejoin_the_European_Union.svg/1280px-Opinion_polling_on_the_whether_the_United_Kingdom_should_rejoin_the_European_Union.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Opinion_polling_on_the_whether_the_United_Kingdom_should_rejoin_the_European_Union.svg)

A chart of showing polling since January 2020 on whether the United Kingdom should rejoin the European Union or not: rejoin support became more prevalent over Brexit support after one year and a half and kept increasing over time, reaching a supermajority in mid-2025, while support for staying out of the EU sharply dropped in 2022 and remained poor in the following years, failing to reach the threshold of 40% of support in polls since the spring of 2022.

## No-deal planning

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: No-deal planning")]

See also: [No-deal Brexit](/wiki/No-deal_Brexit "No-deal Brexit") and [Operation Yellowhammer](/wiki/Operation_Yellowhammer "Operation Yellowhammer")

On 19 December 2018, the EU Commission revealed its "no-deal" Contingency Action Plan in specific sectors, in respect of the UK leaving the EU "in 100 days' time."[[226]](#cite_note-227)

In the wake of the United Kingdom's vote to leave the European Union, the [Department for International Trade](/wiki/Department_for_International_Trade "Department for International Trade") (DIT) for reaching and extending trade agreements between the UK and non-EU states was created by Prime Minister May, shortly after she took office on 13 July 2016.[[227]](#cite_note-csw_new_article-228) By 2017, it employed about 200 trade negotiators[[228]](#cite_note-economist2017-229) and was overseen by then [Secretary of State for International Trade](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_International_Trade "Secretary of State for International Trade") [Liam Fox](/wiki/Liam_Fox "Liam Fox"). In March 2019, the British government announced that it would cut many import tariffs to zero, in the event of a no-deal Brexit.[[229]](#cite_note-230) The [Confederation of British Industry](/wiki/Confederation_of_British_Industry "Confederation of British Industry") said the move would be a "sledgehammer for our economy",[[230]](#cite_note-Guardian2019mar13-231)[[231]](#cite_note-232)[[232]](#cite_note-233) and the [National Farmer's Union](/wiki/National_Farmers%27_Union_of_England_and_Wales "National Farmers' Union of England and Wales") was also highly critical.[[233]](#cite_note-234) Additionally, the plan appeared to breach standard WTO rules.[[234]](#cite_note-235)[[235]](#cite_note-236)[[236]](#cite_note-237)

On 2 June 2020, Chancellor of Germany [Angela Merkel](/wiki/Angela_Merkel "Angela Merkel") stated that the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") must prepare for the possible failure of Brexit trade talks with the [UK](/wiki/UK "UK"). She added that negotiations were being accelerated to try and reach a deal that could be ratified by the end of the year. Her warning came as the deadline for extending talks passed, with negotiations expected to end on 31 December with or without a deal.[[237]](#cite_note-238)

## Litigation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Litigation")]

There has been litigation to explore the constitutional footings on which Brexit stands after *[R (Miller) v Secretary of State for Exiting the European Union](/wiki/R_(Miller)_v_Secretary_of_State_for_Exiting_the_European_Union "R (Miller) v Secretary of State for Exiting the European Union")* (simply known as the "Miller case") and the 2017 Notification Act:

* In *R. (Webster) v Secretary of State for Exiting the European Union*, a [divisional court](/wiki/Divisional_court_(England_and_Wales) "Divisional court (England and Wales)") of [Gross LJ](/wiki/Peter_Gross_(judge) "Peter Gross (judge)") and [Green MR](/wiki/Nicholas_Green_(judge) "Nicholas Green (judge)") determined that the substantive decision to leave the EU that was notified on 29 March 2017 was in fact the executive decision of the Prime Minister using a statutory power of decision found to have been delegated to her by the Notification Act: this is confirmed by the [House of Commons Library](/wiki/House_of_Commons_Library "House of Commons Library") commentary on the case.[[238]](#cite_note-Miller-239) The case was appealed to the [Court of Appeal](/wiki/Court_of_Appeal_(England_and_Wales) "Court of Appeal (England and Wales)")[[239]](#cite_note-240) and paragraph 15 of the judgement, along with the citable nature of the decision were upheld. While the case was criticised academically by Robert Craig, who lectures in jurisprudence at the London School of Economics,[[240]](#cite_note-241) aspects of the case's analysis were supported by the [Supreme Court](/wiki/UK_Supreme_Court "UK Supreme Court") in [Miller 2](/wiki/Miller_2 "Miller 2") at paragraph 57, which confirmed:

> ... that Parliament, and in particular the House of Commons as the democratically elected representatives of the people, has a right to have a voice in how that change comes about is indisputable.[[241]](#cite_note-242)

— Supreme Court of the United Kingdom (UKSC/2019/41)

* This confirmation that the decision was an executive act was part of the basis of [*R.* (*Wilson) v. Prime Minister*](/wiki/R_(Wilson)_v_Prime_Minister "R (Wilson) v Prime Minister")[[242]](#cite_note-243) which allied this point with the concerns about the irregularities in the referendum. The High Court hearing was on 7 December 2018 before [Ouseley MJ](/wiki/Duncan_Ouseley "Duncan Ouseley")[[243]](#cite_note-244) and when judgement was given it was held that: courts' job was not to rule on irregularities in the 'leave' campaign as these were not questions of law; it was also said that the case was brought both too early and too late.[[238]](#cite_note-Miller-239) Judgement in the Court of Appeal (before [Hickinbottom LJ](/wiki/Gary_Hickinbottom "Gary Hickinbottom") and [Haddon-Cave LJ](/wiki/Charles_Haddon-Cave "Charles Haddon-Cave")) before also went against the applicant.[[244]](#cite_note-245)
* Regarding the reversibility of a notification under Article 50, *Wightman and others v Secretary of State for Exiting the European Union* was referred to the [Court of Justice of the European Union](/wiki/Court_of_Justice_of_the_European_Union "Court of Justice of the European Union");[[245]](#cite_note-246) the UK government sought to block this referral, taking the matter on appeal to the Supreme Court, but was unsuccessful.[[246]](#cite_note-247) On 10 December 2018, the Court of Justice of the EU ruled that the UK could unilaterally revoke its Article 50 notification.[[247]](#cite_note-248)

## Impact

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Impact")]

"Impact of Brexit" redirects here. For predicted impact, see [Predicted impact of Brexit](/wiki/Predicted_impact_of_Brexit "Predicted impact of Brexit").

Many effects of Brexit depended on whether the UK left with a [withdrawal agreement](/wiki/Withdrawal_agreement "Withdrawal agreement"), or before an agreement was ratified (["no-deal" Brexit](/wiki/No-deal_Brexit "No-deal Brexit")).[[248]](#cite_note-What_is_a_'no_deal'_Brexit?-249) In 2017, the *Financial Times* said that there were approximately 759 international agreements, spanning 168 non-EU countries, that the UK would no longer be a party to upon leaving the EU.[[249]](#cite_note-FTAfter-250)

### Economic effects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Economic effects")]

Main article: [Economic effects of Brexit](/wiki/Economic_effects_of_Brexit "Economic effects of Brexit")

Economists speculated that Brexit would have a damaging impact on the economies of the UK and at least part of the EU27. In particular, there was a broad consensus among economists and in the economic literature that Brexit would likely reduce the UK's real per capita income in the medium and long term, and that the Brexit referendum itself would damage the economy.[[74]](#cite_note-Sampson-2017-75)[[250]](#cite_note-Giles-2017-251)[[251]](#cite_note-252) Studies found Brexit-induced uncertainty reduced British GDP, British national income, investment by business, employment, and British international trade from June 2016 onwards.[[252]](#cite_note-253)[[253]](#cite_note-254)[[254]](#cite_note-255)

A 2019 analysis found that British firms substantially increased [offshoring](/wiki/Offshoring "Offshoring") to the EU after the Brexit referendum, whereas European firms reduced new investments in the UK.[[255]](#cite_note-256)[[256]](#cite_note-257) The British government's Brexit analysis, leaked in January 2018, showed British economic growth would be stunted by 2–8% over the 15 years following Brexit, the amount depending on the leave scenario.[[257]](#cite_note-258)[[258]](#cite_note-259) Economists warned that London's future as an international financial centre depended on passport agreements with the EU.[[259]](#cite_note-260)[[260]](#cite_note-261) Pro-Brexit activists and politicians have argued for negotiating trade and migration agreements with the "[CANZUK](/wiki/CANZUK "CANZUK")" countries—those of [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") and the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom")[[261]](#cite_note-262)[[262]](#cite_note-263)—but economists have said that trade deals with those countries would be far less valuable to the UK than EU membership.[[263]](#cite_note-264)[[264]](#cite_note-265)[[265]](#cite_note-266) Studies projected that Brexit would exacerbate regional economic inequality in the UK, by hitting already-struggling regions the hardest.[[266]](#cite_note-267)

On 11 January 2024, the London Mayor's Office released "*Mayor highlights Brexit damage to London economy*".[[267]](#cite_note-LMO-268) The release cites the independent report by Cambridge Econometrics that London has almost 300,000 fewer jobs, and nationwide two million fewer jobs as a direct consequence of Brexit.[[267]](#cite_note-LMO-268) Brexit is recognised as a key contributor to the 2023 cost-of-living crisis, with the average citizen being nearly £2,000 worse off, and the average Londoner nearly £3,400 worse off in 2023 as a result of Brexit.[[267]](#cite_note-LMO-268) In addition, UK real Gross Value Added was approximately £140bn less in 2023 than it would have been had the UK remained in the Single Market.[[267]](#cite_note-LMO-268)

In 2024, French customs considered Brexit, in the meantime, has reduced trade between the UK and the EU, but increased trade between China and the United Kingdom.[[268]](#cite_note-269)

### Local and geographic effects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Local and geographic effects")]

Main article: [Post-Brexit United Kingdom relations with the European Union](/wiki/Post-Brexit_United_Kingdom_relations_with_the_European_Union "Post-Brexit United Kingdom relations with the European Union")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/The_Border_on_Killeen_School_Road_-_geograph.org.uk_-_446719.jpg/250px-The_Border_on_Killeen_School_Road_-_geograph.org.uk_-_446719.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Border_on_Killeen_School_Road_-_geograph.org.uk_-_446719.jpg)

Border crossing at Killeen (near [Newry](/wiki/Newry "Newry") in [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland")), marked only by a speed limit in km/h (Northern Ireland uses mph.)

The potential [impact on the border](/wiki/Brexit_and_the_Irish_border "Brexit and the Irish border") between [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland") and the [Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland") has been a contentious issue. Since 2005, [the border](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland%E2%80%93United_Kingdom_border "Republic of Ireland–United Kingdom border") had been essentially invisible.[[269]](#cite_note-270) After Brexit, it became the only UK–EU land border[[270]](#cite_note-271) (not counting the land borders that EU states Spain and Cyprus have with [British Overseas Territories](/wiki/British_Overseas_Territories "British Overseas Territories")). All involved parties agreed a hard border should be avoided,[[271]](#cite_note-272) because it might compromise the [Good Friday Agreement](/wiki/Good_Friday_Agreement "Good Friday Agreement") that ended the [Northern Ireland conflict](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_conflict "Northern Ireland conflict").[[272]](#cite_note-273)[[273]](#cite_note-274)[[274]](#cite_note-275) To forestall this, the EU proposed a ["backstop agreement"](/wiki/Irish_backstop "Irish backstop") that would keep the UK in the Customs Union and keep Northern Ireland in some aspects of the Single Market until a lasting solution was found.[[275]](#cite_note-BBC_backstop-276) The UK Parliament rejected this proposal. After [further negotiations in autumn of 2019](/wiki/Brexit_negotiations_in_2019 "Brexit negotiations in 2019"), an alternative model, the [Ireland/Northern Ireland Protocol](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Protocol "Northern Ireland Protocol") was agreed between the UK and the EU. Under the Protocol, Northern Ireland is formally outside the EU single market, but [EU free movement of goods](/wiki/European_Single_Market "European Single Market") rules and [EU Customs Union](/wiki/EU_Customs_Union "EU Customs Union") rules still apply; this ensures there are no customs checks or controls between Northern Ireland and the rest of the island. In place of an Ireland/Northern Ireland land border, the protocol has created a *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* customs "[Irish Sea border](/wiki/Irish_Sea_border "Irish Sea border")" for goods from (but not to) Great Britain,[[276]](#cite_note-bbcNewDeal-277)[[277]](#cite_note-FTNewDeal-278) to the disquiet of prominent [Unionists](/wiki/Ulster_Unionism "Ulster Unionism").[[278]](#cite_note-279)

After the Brexit referendum, the [Scottish Government](/wiki/Scottish_Government "Scottish Government") – led by the [Scottish National Party](/wiki/Scottish_National_Party "Scottish National Party") (SNP) – planned [another independence referendum](/wiki/Proposed_second_Scottish_independence_referendum "Proposed second Scottish independence referendum") because Scotland voted to remain in the EU while England and Wales voted to leave.[[279]](#cite_note-280) It had suggested this before the Brexit referendum.[[280]](#cite_note-281) The [First Minister of Scotland](/wiki/First_Minister_of_Scotland "First Minister of Scotland"), [Nicola Sturgeon](/wiki/Nicola_Sturgeon "Nicola Sturgeon"), requested a referendum be held before the UK's withdrawal,[[281]](#cite_note-282) but the British prime minister rejected this timing, but not the referendum itself.[[282]](#cite_note-283) At the referendum in 2014, 55% of voters had decided to remain in the UK, but the referendum on Britain's withdrawal from the EU was held in 2016, with 62% of Scottish voters against it. In March 2017, the Scottish Parliament voted in favour of holding another independence referendum. Sturgeon called for a "phased return" of an independent Scotland back to the EU.[[283]](#cite_note-284) In 2017, if Northern Ireland remained associated with the EU – for example, by remaining in the Customs Union – some analysts argued Scotland would also insist on special treatment.[[284]](#cite_note-Brexit-Reuters-04122017-285) However, in that event, the only part of the United Kingdom which would receive unique treatment was Northern Ireland.[[285]](#cite_note-286)

On 21 March 2018, the Scottish Parliament passed the [Scottish Continuity Bill](/wiki/UK_Withdrawal_from_the_European_Union_(Legal_Continuity)_(Scotland)_Bill_2018 "UK Withdrawal from the European Union (Legal Continuity) (Scotland) Bill 2018").[[286]](#cite_note-Herald_Scotland-287) This was passed due to stalling negotiations between the Scottish Government and the British Government on where powers within devolved policy areas should lie after Brexit. The Act allowed for all devolved policy areas to remain within the remit of the Scottish Parliament and reduced the executive power upon exit day that the UK Withdrawal Bill provides for Ministers of the Crown.[[287]](#cite_note-288) The bill was referred to the UK Supreme Court, which found that it could not come into force as the [European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018](/wiki/European_Union_(Withdrawal)_Act_2018 "European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018"), which received royal assent between the Scottish Parliament passing its bill and the Supreme Court's judgement, designated itself under schedule 4 of the [Scotland Act 1998](/wiki/Scotland_Act_1998 "Scotland Act 1998") as unamendable by the Scottish Parliament.[[288]](#cite_note-289) The bill has therefore not received royal assent.[[289]](#cite_note-290)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Gibraltar_Customs_side_of_the_Spain-Gibraltar_frontier%2C_Winston_Churchill_Avenue%2C_Gibraltar.jpg/250px-Gibraltar_Customs_side_of_the_Spain-Gibraltar_frontier%2C_Winston_Churchill_Avenue%2C_Gibraltar.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gibraltar_Customs_side_of_the_Spain-Gibraltar_frontier,_Winston_Churchill_Avenue,_Gibraltar.jpg)

Cars crossing into [Gibraltar](/wiki/Gibraltar "Gibraltar") from [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain") clearing customs formalities. Gibraltar is outside the customs union, VAT area, and [Schengen Zone](/wiki/Schengen_Zone "Schengen Zone").

[Gibraltar](/wiki/Gibraltar "Gibraltar"), a [British Overseas Territory](/wiki/British_Overseas_Territory "British Overseas Territory") bordering Spain, is [affected by Brexit](/wiki/Effect_of_Brexit_on_Gibraltar "Effect of Brexit on Gibraltar") too. Spain asserts a [territorial claim on Gibraltar](/wiki/Status_of_Gibraltar "Status of Gibraltar"). After the referendum, Spain's Foreign Minister renewed calls for joint Spanish–British control.[[290]](#cite_note-291) In late 2018, the British and Spanish governments agreed that any dispute over Gibraltar would not affect Brexit negotiations,[[291]](#cite_note-292) and the British government agreed that UK–EU treaties made after Brexit would not automatically apply to Gibraltar.[[292]](#cite_note-293) In December 2020, Spain and the UK reached an [agreement in principle on future arrangements](/wiki/Effect_of_Brexit_on_Gibraltar#Post-Brexit "Effect of Brexit on Gibraltar") for Brexit and invited the European Commission to formalise it as a treaty.

The French and British governments say they remain committed to the [Le Touquet Agreement](/wiki/Le_Touquet_Agreement "Le Touquet Agreement"), which lets UK border checks be completed in France, and vice versa ([juxtaposed controls](/wiki/Juxtaposed_controls "Juxtaposed controls")).[[293]](#cite_note-French_border-294) The two governments signed the Sandhurst Treaty in January 2018, which will shorten the time taken to process [migrants attempting to reach the UK](/wiki/Migrants_around_Calais "Migrants around Calais") from [Calais](/wiki/Calais "Calais"), from six months to one month. The UK also announced it will invest a further £44.5 million on border security at the English Channel.[[293]](#cite_note-French_border-294)

### Effects on the European Union

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Effects on the European Union")]

Main article: [Impact of Brexit on the European Union](/wiki/Impact_of_Brexit_on_the_European_Union "Impact of Brexit on the European Union")

Brexit [caused the European Union](/wiki/Impact_of_Brexit_on_the_European_Union "Impact of Brexit on the European Union") to lose its second-largest economy, its third-most populous country,[[294]](#cite_note-295) and the second-largest net contributor to the EU budget.[[295]](#cite_note-296)

The UK is no longer a shareholder in the [European Investment Bank](/wiki/European_Investment_Bank "European Investment Bank"), where it had 16% of the shares.[[296]](#cite_note-297) The European Investment Bank's Board of Governors decided that the remaining member states would proportionally increase their capital subscriptions to maintain the same level of overall subscribed capital (EUR 243.3 billion).[[297]](#cite_note-298) As of March 2020, the subscribed capital of the EIB had increased by an additional EUR 5.5 billion, following the decision by two member states to increase their capital subscriptions ([Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") and [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania")). The EIB's total subscribed capital thus amounted to EUR 248.8 billion. Brexit did not impact the EIB Group's AAA credit rating.[[298]](#cite_note-299)

Analyses indicated that the departure of the relatively economically liberal UK would reduce the ability of remaining economically liberal countries to block measures in the [Council of the EU](/wiki/Council_of_the_EU "Council of the EU").[[299]](#cite_note-300)[[300]](#cite_note-301) In 2019, ahead of Brexit, the [European Medicines Agency](/wiki/European_Medicines_Agency "European Medicines Agency") and [European Banking Authority](/wiki/European_Banking_Authority "European Banking Authority") moved their headquarters from London to [Amsterdam](/wiki/Amsterdam "Amsterdam") and [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris"), respectively.[[301]](#cite_note-302)[[302]](#cite_note-303)[[303]](#cite_note-304)

### Sectorial effects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Sectorial effects")]

#### Academia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Academia")]

Brexit poses challenges to British academia and research, as the UK loses research funding from EU sources and sees a reduction in students from the EU. Academic institutions find it harder to hire researchers from the EU and British students will face greater difficulties with studying abroad in the EU.[[304]](#cite_note-Mayhew-2017-305) The UK was a member of the [European Research Area](/wiki/European_Research_Area "European Research Area") and likely to wish to remain an associated member following Brexit.[[305]](#cite_note-306) The British government has guaranteed funding for research currently funded by EU.[[306]](#cite_note-307)

#### Aviation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Aviation")]

By leaving the EU, the UK would leave the [European Common Aviation Area](/wiki/European_Common_Aviation_Area "European Common Aviation Area") (ECAA), a [single market](/wiki/Single_market "Single market") in commercial air travel,[[307]](#cite_note-Aviation-IFG-308) but could negotiate a number of different future relationships with the EU.[[307]](#cite_note-Aviation-IFG-308) British airlines would still have permission to operate within the EU with no restrictions, and vice versa. The British government seeks continued participation in the [European Aviation Safety Agency](/wiki/European_Aviation_Safety_Agency "European Aviation Safety Agency") (EASA).[[307]](#cite_note-Aviation-IFG-308) The UK has its own [air service agreements](/wiki/Air_transport_agreement "Air transport agreement") with 111 countries, which permit flights to-and-from the country, and further 17 countries through its EU membership.[[308]](#cite_note-309) These have since been replaced. Ferries will continue, but with obstacles such as customs checks.[[309]](#cite_note-atclogistics-310) New ferry departures between the Republic of Ireland and the European mainland have been established.[[309]](#cite_note-atclogistics-310) As of August 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit), the government's [Goods Vehicle Movement Service](/wiki/Goods_Vehicle_Movement_Service "Goods Vehicle Movement Service"), an IT system essential to post-Brexit goods movements, was still only in the early stages of beta testing, with four months to go before it is required to be in operation.[[310]](#cite_note-311)

#### Finance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Finance")]

A 2025 report estimated that Brexit has caused a £20 billion productivity decline in the UK financial sector, as major firms shifted staff and assets to EU cities such as Frankfurt and Paris. Policymakers are exploring measures to revive competitiveness outside the single market.[[311]](#cite_note-312)

#### Fishing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Fishing")]

The UK has left the [Common Agricultural Policy](/wiki/Common_Agricultural_Policy "Common Agricultural Policy") (CAP),[[312]](#cite_note-CAP_payments-313) which provides government financial support to farmers in the EU.[[313]](#cite_note-CAP-314) Brexit allowed the UK to develop its own agriculture policy[[314]](#cite_note-UK_agriculture_policy-315) and the [Agriculture Act 2020](/w/index.php?title=Agriculture_Act_2020&action=edit&redlink=1 "Agriculture Act 2020 (page does not exist)") replaced the CAP with a new system.[[315]](#cite_note-Agriculture_Act_2020-316) The UK also left the [Common Fisheries Policy](/wiki/Common_Fisheries_Policy "Common Fisheries Policy") (CFP)[[316]](#cite_note-fisheries_briefing-317) that lets all EU countries fish within 12 nautical miles of the British coast[[317]](#cite_note-318) and lets the EU set catch quotas.[[318]](#cite_note-CFP_explainer-319) The combined EU fishing fleets landed about six million tonnes of fish per year, as of 2016,[[319]](#cite_note-320) about half of which were from British waters.[[320]](#cite_note-321) By leaving the CFP, the UK could develop its own fisheries policy.[[318]](#cite_note-CFP_explainer-319) The UK did also leave the [London Fisheries Convention](/wiki/London_Fisheries_Convention "London Fisheries Convention") that lets Irish, French, Belgian, Dutch and German vessels fish within six nautical miles of the UK's coast.[[321]](#cite_note-322)

#### Health Services

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Health Services")]

An early 2019 study found that Brexit would deplete the [National Health Service](/wiki/National_Health_Service "National Health Service") (NHS) workforce, create uncertainties regarding care for British nationals living in the EU, and put at risk access to vaccines, equipment, and medicines.[[322]](#cite_note-323) The [Department of Health and Social Care](/wiki/Department_of_Health_and_Social_Care "Department of Health and Social Care") has said it has taken steps to ensure the continuity of medical supplies after Brexit.[[323]](#cite_note-324) The number of non-British EU nurses registering with the NHS fell from 1,304 in July 2016 to 46 in April 2017.[[324]](#cite_note-325)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]In June 2016, 58,702 NHS staff recorded a non-British EU nationality, and in June 2022, 70,735 NHS staff recorded an EU nationality. However, "to present this as the full story would be misleading, because there are over 57,000 more staff for whom nationality is known now than in 2016"[[325]](#cite_note-326)[[326]](#cite_note-327)

There was a concern that a disorderly Brexit might have compromised patients' access to vital medicines.[[327]](#cite_note-328) Pharmaceutical organisations working with the Civil Service to keep medicine supplies available in the case of a no-deal Brexit had to sign 26 [Non-Disclosure Agreements](/wiki/Non-disclosure_agreement "Non-disclosure agreement") (NDAs) to prevent them from giving the public information. The figures were given on 21 December 2018 after [Rushanara Ali](/wiki/Rushanara_Ali "Rushanara Ali") asked a parliamentary question.[[328]](#cite_note-329)

#### Immigration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Immigration")]

Main article: [Modern immigration to the United Kingdom](/wiki/Modern_immigration_to_the_United_Kingdom "Modern immigration to the United Kingdom")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Brexit_migration_chart.svg/500px-Brexit_migration_chart.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Brexit_migration_chart.svg)

UK net migration before and after the withdrawal from the EU.

After Brexit, the UK is able to control immigration from the [European Economic Area](/wiki/European_Economic_Area "European Economic Area") (EU (except Ireland) and [EFTA](/wiki/EFTA "EFTA") countries),[[329]](#cite_note-330) as withdrawal ends UK participation in the EU's [freedom of movement](/wiki/Freedom_of_movement "Freedom of movement") principle – in both directions. The British government of the time proposed to replace it with a new system of immigration control. The government's 2018 [white paper](/wiki/White_paper "White paper") proposes a "skills-based immigration system" that prioritises skilled migrants. EU and EEA citizens already living in the UK can continue living there after Brexit by applying to the EU Settlement Scheme, which began in March 2019. Irish citizens will not have to apply to the scheme.[[330]](#cite_note-331)[[331]](#cite_note-332)[[332]](#cite_note-333) Studies estimate that Brexit and the end of free movement will likely result in a large decline in immigration from EEA countries to the UK.[[333]](#cite_note-immigration_decline-334)[[334]](#cite_note-335) After Brexit, any foreigner wanting to work in the UK would need a work permit.[[335]](#cite_note-336)[[336]](#cite_note-337)

Since the full implementation of Brexit, more [EU nationals](/wiki/Member_state_of_the_European_Union "Member state of the European Union") are leaving the UK than moving to it.[[337]](#cite_note-338) After Brexit, the number of EU citizens who were refused entry to the UK increased fivefold.[[338]](#cite_note-339) Of the 1,218,000 immigrants who came to the UK in 2023, only 126,000 were citizens of EU member states.[[339]](#cite_note-bbc-23-05-2024-340) BBC reported that "In the 12 months to June 2023, net EU migration was -86,000, meaning more EU nationals left the UK than arrived".[[339]](#cite_note-bbc-23-05-2024-340)

#### Law and Security

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Law and Security")]

Under the [European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018](/wiki/European_Union_(Withdrawal)_Act_2018 "European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018"), EU laws will no longer have [supremacy over](/wiki/Primacy_of_European_Union_law "Primacy of European Union law") British laws after Brexit.[[340]](#cite_note-withdrawal_bill-341) To maintain continuity, the Act converts EU law into British law as "retained EU law". After Brexit, the British parliament (and the devolved legislatures) can decide which elements of that law to keep, amend or repeal.[[340]](#cite_note-withdrawal_bill-341) Furthermore, British courts will no longer be bound by the judgments of the [EU Court of Justice](/wiki/EU_Court_of_Justice "EU Court of Justice") after Brexit.

Concerns were raised by European lawmakers, including [Michel Barnier](/wiki/Michel_Barnier "Michel Barnier"), that Brexit might create security problems for the UK given that its law enforcement and counter-terrorism forces would no longer have access to the EU's security databases.[[341]](#cite_note-342)

#### Trade

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Trade")]

Forecasts were made at the time of the referendum that Brexit would impose trade barriers, leading to a decline in trade between the United Kingdom and the European Union; however, after a dip in 2020 as result of worldwide lockdowns, by 2022 trade in both directions had risen to higher levels than before Brexit. Goods trade had fallen but was outweighed by an increase in professional services.[[342]](#cite_note-343)

Some analysts have suggested that the severe [economic impact of the COVID-19 pandemic in the UK](/wiki/Economic_impact_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_Kingdom "Economic impact of the COVID-19 pandemic in the United Kingdom") has masked the economic impact of Brexit in 2021.[[343]](#cite_note-344) In December 2021, the *Financial Times* quoted a range of economists as saying that the economic impact of Brexit on the UK economy and living standards "appears to be negative but uncertain".[[344]](#cite_note-345) According to the [Office for Budget Responsibility](/wiki/Office_for_Budget_Responsibility "Office for Budget Responsibility"), the new trade agreement between the EU and UK could, over time, result in a 4% reduction in British productivity, compared with its level had the 2016 EU referendum gone the other way.[[345]](#cite_note-346)

#### Utilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Utilities")]

Brexit was widely described as a factor contributing to the [2021 United Kingdom natural gas supplier crisis](/wiki/2021_United_Kingdom_natural_gas_supplier_crisis "2021 United Kingdom natural gas supplier crisis"), in which [panic buying](/wiki/2021_United_Kingdom_fuel_supply_crisis "2021 United Kingdom fuel supply crisis") led to serious disruption of road fuel supplies across the UK, as it exacerbated the UK's shortage of [HGV](/wiki/Heavy_goods_vehicle "Heavy goods vehicle") drivers.[[346]](#cite_note-347)[[347]](#cite_note-bbc-202107-348)[[348]](#cite_note-349) In a July 2021 report, the [Road Haulage Association](/wiki/Road_Haulage_Association "Road Haulage Association") estimated the UK faced a shortage of up to 100,000 truck drivers.[[349]](#cite_note-350)[[350]](#cite_note-351)[[351]](#cite_note-352)

## Cultural references

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brexit&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Cultural references")]

Main article: [Brexit in popular culture](/wiki/Brexit_in_popular_culture "Brexit in popular culture")

Brexit has inspired many creative works, such as murals, sculptures, novels, plays, music, movies and video games. The response of British artists and writers to Brexit has in general been negative, reflecting a reported overwhelming percentage of people involved in Britain's [creative industries](/wiki/Creative_industries "Creative industries") voting against leaving the European Union.[[352]](#cite_note-353) Despite issues around immigration being central in the Brexit debate, British artists left the migrants' perspective largely unexplored. However, Brexit also inspired UK-based migrant artists to create new works and "claim agency over their representation within public spaces and create a platform for a new social imagination that can facilitate transnational and trans-local encounters, multicultural democratic spaces, sense of commonality, and solidarity."[[353]](#cite_note-354)

(Article 50(3) of the [Treaty on European Union](/wiki/Treaty_on_European_Union "Treaty on European Union") states: "The Treaties shall cease to apply to the State in question from the date of entry into force of the withdrawal agreement or, failing that, two years after the notification referred to in paragraph 2, unless the European Council, in agreement with the Member State concerned, unanimously decides to extend this period".)

249. **[^](#cite_ref-FTAfter_250-0)** McClean, Paul (30 May 2017). ["After Brexit: the UK will need to renegotiate at least 759 treaties"](https://www.ft.com/content/f1435a8e-372b-11e7-bce4-9023f8c0fd2e). *Financial Times*. [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20170531162500/https://www.ft.com/content/f1435a8e-372b-11e7-bce4-9023f8c0fd2e) from the original on 31 May 2017. Retrieved 31 May 2017. "Through analysis of the EU treaty database, the FT found 759 separate EU bilateral agreements with potential relevance to Britain, covering trade in nuclear goods, customs, fisheries, trade, transport and regulatory co-operation in areas such as antitrust or financial services. This includes multilateral agreements based on consensus, where Britain must re-approach 132 separate parties. Around 110 separate opt-in accords at the UN and World Trade Organization are excluded from the estimates, as are narrow agreements on the environment, health, research and science. Some additional UK bilateral deals, outside the EU framework, may also need to be revised because they make reference to EU law. Some of the 759 are so essential that it would be unthinkable to operate without them. Air services agreements allow British aeroplanes to land in America, Canada or Israel; nuclear accords permit the trade in spare parts and fuel for Britain's power stations. Both these sectors are excluded from trade negotiations and must be addressed separately."
